questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs with reference to the Indus Valley Civilisation:
Site Major Finding
I. - Banawali Terracotta Model of Plough
II. - Kalibangan Multi Cropping
III. - Chanhudaro Bead-making industry
IV. - Mohenjodaro Statue of the priest-king
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Indus Valley Civilization (IVC), was a **Bronze Age civilization** that flourished in the northwestern regions of South Asia, primarily along the basins of the Indus River and the now-dry Ghaggar-Hakra River.
● At **Banawali (Haryana)**, the early Harappan phase was marked by mud-brick houses with hearths and plastered storage pits in the courtyards. **Terracotta models of the plough** have been found at sites in Cholistan and at Banawali. However, no actual ploughs have survived, because they were made of wood. **Several houses at Banawali gave evidence of fire altars. So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Kalibangan, Rajasthan** gets its name from the thick clusters of black bangles lying all over the surface of its mounds. This site lies on the banks of the dry bed of the **Ghaggar river.** Archaeologists have found evidence of a ploughed field at Kalibangan. The field had two sets of furrows at right angles to each other, suggesting that **two** **different crops were grown together**. **So, pair II is correctly** **matched.**
● **Chanhudaro (Pakistan) does not have fortifications**. There are mud-brick platforms with remains of various structures. Chanhudaro was an important centre of craft activity.
o Some of the houses yielded raw material such as **carnelian, agate, amethyst, and crystal** as well as finished and unfinished beads and drills. More striking was the discovery of a **bead factory**, with lots of finished and unfinished beads, mostly made of steatite.
o Seal making, shell working, and the making of stone weights seem to have been other important crafts practised here. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Mohenjodaro** (Pakistan) lies on the banks of the **Indus** river. It is well known for the discovery of a male figure which has been labelled the **‘priest-king’.** It's a **steatite** sculpture of a bearded man, possibly wearing a headband and a cloak decorated with patterns. **So, pair IV is correctly matched.** **So, all four of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText: The famous “curry stones” of the Harappan Civilization were primarily used for:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Excavations at Mohenjodaro**, a prominent Harappan site, revealed saddle querns, commonly called “curry stones”, which were used as **grinding tools**. These stones were made of hard, gritty rock or sandstone, and were used in two distinct ways:
○ One type involved rolling a smaller stone to and fro — likely used for grinding cereals like wheat or barley. ○ The other type used a pounding action — possibly for pounding herbs and spices, hence the nickname “curry stones”.
● These **were not ornamental or industrial tools but kitchen implements reflecting early food** **processing practices**.
● This finding illustrates how the Harappan domestic economy included advanced techniques for food preparation, using simple but effective stone tools. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Nageshwar
II. 	 Balakot
III.	 Kot Diji
How many of the above were the craft sites of the Indus Valley Civilisation?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Important Craft Sites of the  Indus Valley Civilisation:**
● **Nageshwar:** It is located on the southern shore of the Gulf of Kutch in Gujarat, India. ○ It is primarily recognized as a **highly specialized shell-working site**. ○ Its strategic coastal location provided easy access to marine shells, a crucial raw material for various crafts. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Balakot:** It is situated on the Makran coast of Balochistan, **Pakistan**. ○ Its coastal position facilitated maritime activities and trade. It was a **prominent center for shell** **manufacturing. So, point II is correct.**
● **Kot Diji:** It is known for distinctive pottery, often a fine, **red-slipped ware** with painted designs. **So,** **point III is correct.** **So, all three** of the above were the craft sites of the  Indus Valley Civilisation. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Sun-dried bricks
II. 	 Wood
III. 	Mortar of Gypsum
IV. 	 Iron clamps
How many of the above materials were used in the construction of buildings during the Indus Valley Civilization?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The cities in the Indus Valley Civilisation (IVC) were planned and used various materials for construction. Houses were often multi-storied and built around courtyards.
● People of **Harappan civilization** had extensively used bricks for urban planning and settlement, which, whether **sun-dried or baked**, were of a standardised ratio, where the length and breadth were four times and twice the height respectively. Such bricks were used at all Harappan settlements. **So, point I is** **correct.**
● The **mortar of gypsum was used in the construction of the Great Bath.** It was a large rectangular tank in a courtyard of the citadel, surrounded by a corridor on all four sides. There were two flights of steps on the north and south leading into the tank, which was made **watertight by setting bricks on edge and** **using a mortar of gypsum**. **So, point III is correct.**
● The **wood was used for the construction of roofs**. The people of Harappan civilization lived in houses of different sizes, mostly consisting of rooms arranged around a central courtyard. Doorways and windows generally faced the side lanes and rarely opened onto the main streets. The view from the lane into the courtyard was blocked off by a wall. **Floors** were usually made of **hard-packed earth**, often re-plastered or covered with sand. The ceilings were probably over 3 m high. **So, point II is correct.**
● The Indus Valley Civilization was a Bronze Age civilization, flourishing roughly between 2600 BCE and 1900 BCE. **Iron technology was unknown to the Harappans.** The Iron Age in the Indian subcontinent began much later, typically after 1800-1200 BCE, long after the decline of the IVC. Their tools and implements were primarily made of copper and bronze. **So, point IV is not correct.** **So, only three of the above materials were used in the construction of buildings during the Indus Valley** **Civilization.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Chalcolithic cultures in India:
I. 	 The Ganeshwar Jodhpura culture is notable
for its copper artifacts.
II. 	 The Ahar culture is recognized for large-scale
production of ceramics.
III. 	The Malwa culture is associated with
distinctive cream-slipped ware.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Different regions of the Indian subcontinent experienced the emergence of numerous regional cultures by the second millennium B.C., which were distinguished by the use of copper and stone tools. Thus, these societies are known as **chalcolithic societies.**
● The **Ganeshwar–Jodhpura culture**, dating to the 4th–3rd millennium BCE, was first identified at **Jodhpura**, a mound on the banks of the Sahibi (Sabi) River in northeast **Rajasthan**. Later, similar pottery—wheel-made, orange to deep-red with incised designs and forms like dish-on-stand—was found at Ganeshwar.
○ The site gained significance following the **discovery of a large number of copper artefacts,** **especially a distinct arrowhead type**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **Ahar culture** is divided into four phases: Early Ahar/Balathal, Transitional Phase, Mature Ahar, and Late Ahar. The early Ahar phase identified at Balathal includes mud and mud brick houses, eight types of **mass production of ceramics**, metalworking, and bead industries developed during this phase. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● **Malwa Culture** is a prominent chalcolithic culture in central India and widely distributed throughout the Malwa region flourished between 1900-1400 BC. It was first identified by the **excavations at Maheshwar** **(on the bank of Narmada)**, which is the ancient Mahishmati. Malwa culture sites flourished mostly on the banks of the tributaries of Narmada. It exhibited following findings:
○ Various pottery types were discovered, including the distinctive Malwa ware, **cream slipware** and red/grey ware.  **So, statement III is correct.** ○ Copper tools and exquisitely made copper axes were found, along with a specialised blade industry. ○ Ornaments, such as bead necklaces made from semi-precious stones, were also unearthed. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText: Mesopotamian texts refer to Meluhha as a land of seafarers and they mention the products from Meluhha: carnelian, lapis lazuli, copper, gold, and varieties of wood. In the above context the place Meluhha was most probably used for which one of the following regions?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Mesopotamian texts mention contacts with the region Meluhha**. They mention the products from Meluhha: carnelian, lapis lazuli, copper, gold, and varieties of wood. Mesopotamian texts refer to Meluhha as a land of seafarers. The **Harappans were known to be a seafaring people with established port cities** (like Lothal) and extensive maritime trade networks. The term Meluhha was a generic term for areas lying to the east of Mesopotamia, including the **Indus valley**.
● **Mesopotamian texts mention cotton being imported from Meluhha**. Traces of cotton cloth were found at Mohenjodaro, preserved over the centuries due to their being in contact with a corroding silver jar. Several examples of cotton thread and cloth were identified on copper tools.
○ There is literary as well as archaeological evidence for Harappan trade with Mesopotamia. **Mesopotamian records of the time of king Sargon (2334–2279 BCE) refer to ships from the** **lands of Dilmun, Magan, and Meluhha** tied along the quay of the capital city, Akkad. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Which areas were called the Magan and Dilmun?**
● **Mesopotamian** texts datable to the third millennium BCE refer to **copper coming from** **a region called Magan**, **perhaps a name for Oman,** and copper found at Mesopotamian sites also contains traces of nickel.
● Mesopotamian texts mention contacts with regions named **Dilmun** (probably the island of **Bahrain**).

questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Archaeologist Known for
I. - Daya Ram Sahni First Indian DirectorGeneral of the Archeological Survey of India (ASI)
II. - Rakhal Das Banerji
Discovered the Dancing Girl statute in Mohenjo Daro
III. - R.E.M. Wheeler Authoured ‘The Story of Indian Archaeology’
IV. - Sir John Hubert Marshall
Discovery of the  site of Harappa
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **First Light on a Long-forgotten Civilisation: New Discoveries of an Unknown Prehistoric Past**, the article authored by **John Marshall**, then-Director General of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI), **announced the discovery of what he called the “civilisation of the Indus Valley”**.
● Two ASI archaeologists were instrumental in the discovery, and were credited by Marshall in his article. **Daya Ram Sahni first excavated Harappa in 1921-22, finding seals, painted pottery, and beads**. **So,** **pair IV is not correctly matched.**
● Known as an “industrious, accurate and modest” man, Sahni later became the **ASI’s first Indian** **Director-General**. The other key player was **Rakhal Das Banerji**. In 1922, he started excavating Mohenjo-daro, also located in modern-day Pakistan, and found seals, pottery, copper products, and crucibles at that site. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
○ The **first Director-General of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) was Sir Alexander** **Cunningham**. He was appointed in 1861 when the ASI was founded. Cunningham is often referred to as the "Father of Indian Archaeology”.
● Marshall was struck by the uncanny similarity in the objects found at Harappa and Mohenjo-daro, although the two sites were 640 km apart. Marshall interpreted the similarities and **announced the discovery of** **the “civilisation of the Indus Valley” in the London newspaper**.
● R.E.M. Wheeler was a British archaeologist and served as the Director General of the ASI in 1944. **'The** **Story of Indian Archaeology' was authoured by S.N. Roy.** It highlights the efforts of various archaeologists, both Indian and foreign, who played a crucial role in uncovering India's ancient civilizations and historical sites. The book discusses key excavations, interpretations of artifacts, and the institutional growth of archaeology in the country. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
● The Dancing Girl figurine from Mohenjo-daro was discovered by British archaeologist **Ernest Mackay** in 1926. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.** **So, only one  of the pairs given above is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Dhamma policy of Ashoka:
I. 	 Ashoka equated Dhamma with Buddhist
teachings.
II. 	 It signified a general code of conduct.
III. 	It urged for conquest through Dhamma
instead of through war.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Ashoka’s Dhamma Policy:** Although Ashoka embraced Buddhism and took efforts to spread Buddhism, **his policy of Dhamma was still a** **broad concept.** It was a way of life, a code of conduct and a set of principles to be adopted and practiced by the people at large. His principles of Dhamma were clearly stated in his Edicts. The main features of Ashoka’s Dhamma as mentioned in his various Edicts may be summed as follows:
● **Service to father and mother**, practice of ahimsa, love of truth, reverence to teachers and good treatment of relatives.
● **Prohibition of animal sacrifices** and festive gatherings and avoiding expensive and meaningless ceremonies and rituals.
● **Efficient organization of administration** in the direction of social welfare and maintenance of constant contact with people through the system of Dhammayatras.
● **Humane treatment of servants** by masters and prisoners by government officials.
● Consideration and **non-violence to animals** and courtesy to relations and liberality to Brahmins.
● **Tolerance** among all the religious sects.
● **Conquest through Dhamma instead of through war. So, statement III is correct.**
● The concept of non-violence and other similar ideas of Asoka s Dhamma are identical with the teachings of Buddha. But he **did not equate Dhamma with Buddhist teachings**. Buddhism remained his personal belief. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● His Dhamma **signifies a general code of conduct**. Ashoka wished that his Dhamma should spread through all social levels. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Subject:** History | Mauryan Empire **Tags:** Factual

questions.questionText:
Which one of the following best describes the
Pashupati Seal discovered from the Indus Valley
Civilization?

options.a:
A horned human figure seated cross-legged
on a platform, surrounded by animals
options.b:
A female figure with a prominent headgear,
seated on a throne
options.c:
A group of human figures engaged in
ritualistic dance around a central fire altar
options.d:
A female deity standing with lions, with birds
perched on her shoulders

questions.explanationMarkdown:
Seals of the Harappan Civilization **are primarily made from steatite**. The purpose of producing seals was mainly **commercial**. It appears that the seals were also used as **amulets**, carried on the persons of their owners, perhaps as modern-day identity cards.
● Attempts have been made to reconstruct religious beliefs and practices prevalent in Indus Valley Civilization, by examining **seals**, some of which seem to depict ritual scenes. Others, with plant motifs, are thought to **indicate nature worship**.
● The **seals of the IVC are famous for their depictions of numerous animals**, both realistic and **mythical** **or composite creatures**. The **unicorn-like creature** is the most frequently depicted animal on IVC seals, often interpreted as a mythical beast. Other seals show composite animals combining features of different beasts, or anthropomorphic figures with animal characteristics. The presence of these fantastic creatures is a distinctive feature of Indus iconography.
● **The Pashupati Seal** depicts a human figure seated cross-legged. An elephant and a tiger are depicted to the right side of the seated figure, while on the left a rhinoceros and a buffalo are seen. In addition to these animals two antelopes are shown below the seat. It has been regarded as a **depiction of “proto-Shiva”,** that is, an early form of one of the major deities of Hinduism. Besides, conical stone objects have been classified as lingas. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements are correct
with respect to the development of crafts in Indus
Valley Civilization (IVC)?
I. 	 The ‘dancing girl’ statue was made using the
lost wax technique.
II. 	 Jasper and Steatite were used in bead making.
III. 	Faience was used to make spindle whorls.
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Dancing Girl** **from Mohenjodaro** is one of the most iconic artifacts of the Indus Valley Civilization, and it is a prime example of the sophisticated metallurgical skill of the Harappans, who utilized the **cire-** **perdue (lost-wax) casting technique** for bronze figures. **The lost-wax method involves** first making a wax model and then covering it with a clay coating, leaving some holes as passageways. When the clay covered molds are heated in ovens, the wax melts out. Molten bronze is then poured in and takes the place of the wax. When the mould has cooled, the outer clay envelope is chipped off and the craftsperson can then put the finishing touches to the solid bronze statue. This technique is still used in certain parts of India. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The beads were made using: **stones** like carnelian (of a beautiful red colour), **jasper**, crystal, quartz and **steatite**; metals like copper, bronze and gold; and shell, faience and terracotta or burnt clay. **So, statement** **II is correct.**
● **Faience** (a material made of ground sand or silica mixed with colour and a gum and then fired) **was used** **to make small ornaments**, beads, bangles, and practical items like **spindle whorls.** Miniature pots of faience, perhaps used as perfume bottles, are found mostly in Mohenjodaro and Harappa. **So, statement** **III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Subject:** History |Indus Valley Civilization **Tags:** Factual

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
The sixth century BCE is often regarded as a major
turning point in early Indian history for which of
the following reasons?
I. 	 Formation of early states
II. 	 Growth of iron usage
III. 	Use of gold coinage
IV. 	 Development of second urbanisation
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In the history of India, the **sixth century BCE is often regarded as a major turning point in early** **Indian history**. It is an era associated with early states, cities, the growing use of iron, the development of coinage, etc. **So, points I and II are correct.**
● The first documented coinage is deemed to start with **'Punch Marked' coins issued between the 7th-6th** **century BC and 1st century AD**. These coins are called 'punch-marked' coins because of their manufacturing technique. Mostly made of silver, these bear symbols, each of which was punched on the coin with a separate punch.
o The **first-ever gold coins in India were issued by the Kushana dynasty**. The first important ruler of the Kushana dynasty was 'Kujula Kadphises' or Kadphises I. **So, point III is not correct.**
● The **second urbanisation** in the Ganga Valley took place during the **6th Century BCE**. This period marked the rise of large cities. **So, point IV is correct.**
● It also witnessed the growth of **diverse systems of thought, including Buddhism and Jainism.** It was a period of the growth of **mahajanapadas**. Early Buddhist and Jaina texts mention, amongst other things, sixteen states known as **mahajanapadas** (territorial developments). Although the lists vary, some names such as Vajji, Magadha, Koshala, Kuru, Panchala, Gandhara and Avanti occur frequently. Clearly, these were amongst the most important mahajanapadas. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.** **Subject**: History | Mahajanapadas **Tags**: Analytical

questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Piyadassi
II. 	 Dhammaraja
III. 	Chakravarti
IV. 	 Shahoanosha
How many of the above titles were used for the
King Ashoka of Mauryan dynasty?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Most scripts used to write modern Indian languages are derived from **Brahmi,** the script used in most Ashokan inscriptions. From the late eighteenth century, European scholars aided by Indian pandits worked backwards from contemporary **Bengali and Devanagari** (the script used to write Hindi) manuscripts, comparing their letters with older specimens.
● Scholars who studied early inscriptions sometimes assumed these were in Sanskrit, although the earliest inscriptions were, in fact, in **Prakrit.** It was only after decades of painstaking investigations by several epigraphists that **James Prinsep was able to decipher Ashokan Brahmi in 1838.**
● It is to be noted that the **name of the ruler, Ashoka** (273 – 232 B.C.), **is not mentioned in the inscription**. What is used instead are titles adopted by the ruler – **devanampiya****,** often translated **as “beloved of the** **gods”** and **piyadassi**, or **“pleasant to behold”** or **“he who regards amiably”.** The name Ashoka is mentioned in some other inscriptions, which also contain these titles. After examining all these inscriptions, and finding that they match in terms of content, style, language and palaeography, epigraphists have concluded that they were issued by the same ruler. **So, point I is correct.**
● King Ashoka also used the title “**Dhammaraja”** before his name. Ashoka is called “**Chakravarti Samrat”**
which means emperor of emperors. Only Emperor Ashoka got this title in India.  **So, points II and  III** **are correct.**
● King **Kanishka,** used the title **“Shahoanosha”** (king of kings) with his name. He was also called **“Devaputra” (Son of gods). So, point IV is not correct.** **So, only three of the above titles were used by the King Ashoka of the Mauryan dynasty.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Mahajanapada period:
I. 	 Gana-Sanghas were monarchies ruled by
hereditary kings.
II. 	 Janapadas were republican assemblies where
decisions were made collectively.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● There were two primary types of Mahajanapadas (kingdoms) during the 6th century BCE: **monarchies** **and republics (also known as ganas or sanghas).** Monarchies were ruled by kings, while republics were governed by a council of representatives, though some republics also had a king-like figurehead.
● While most mahajanapadas were ruled by kings, some, known as ganas or sanghas, were **oligarchies,** where power was **shared by a number of men**, often collectively called rajas. Both **Mahavira and the** **Buddha** belonged to such ganas. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● In some instances, as in the case of the Vajji sangha, the rajas probably controlled resources such as land collectively. Although their histories are often difficult to reconstruct due to the lack of sources, some of these states lasted for nearly a thousand years.
● Janapadas generally referred to territorial states or tribal regions that often evolved into kingdoms under hereditary rulers. They were **not organized as assemblies** like the Gana-Sanghas. **So, statement II is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to Magadha:
**Statement I:**
Magadha emerged as the most powerful among
all the Mahajanapadas of the sixth century BCE.
**Statement II:**
The unorthodox character of the societal set up in
Magadha allowed it to become more receptive to
expansionist policies of its rulers.
**Statement III:**
The Ganga and its tributaries served as vital
waterways for Magadha.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement II and Statement III are
correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement II and Statement III are correct
but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements II and III is correct
and that explains Statement I
options.d:
Neither Statement II nor Statement III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
The mahajanapadas were located over a major part of the Ganga valley with a few to the north-west and southwest. However, of the four most powerful kingdoms, three – **Kosala, the Vajji confederacy and Magadha** — lay in the middle Ganga valley and the fourth, **Avanti** was in western Malwa. The kingdoms that surrounded Magadha were Anga in the east, the Vajji Confederacy to the north, to its immediate west the kingdom of Kashi and further west, the kingdom of Kosala.
● Magadha can be identified with the modern districts of Patna, Gaya, Nalanda and parts of Shahabad in **the present-day state of** **Bihar**. Between the sixth and the fourth centuries BCE, **Magadha** became the most powerful mahajanapada. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Modern historians explain this development in a variety of ways:**
o Geographically, Magadha s location was such that it had in its vicinity large tracts of **alluvial soil.** The soil could be easily cleared off the heavy overgrowth with the use of iron implements and proved extremely fertile. Various varieties of **paddy** were grown as mentioned in the early Buddhist texts. This enabled the farmers to produce considerable surplus which augmented the taxes. Magadha was a region where **agriculture** was especially productive. o Besides, **iron mines (in present-day Jharkhand)** were accessible and provided resources for tools and weapons. o The **unorthodox character of the societal set up** in Magadha allowed it to become more receptive to expansionist policies of its rulers. Magadha had a mixture of Vedic and non-Vedic people who were different in their outlook than those of orthodox Vedic societies. **So, statement II is correct.** o **Elephants,** an important component of the army, were found in forests in the region. o Also, the **Ganga and its tributaries** provided a means of cheap and convenient communication. **So, statement III is correct.** **So, both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● Initially, **Rajagriha** (the Prakrit name for present day Rajgir in Bihar) was the capital of Magadha. Interestingly, the old name means **“house of the king”**. Rajagriha was a fortified settlement, located amongst hills.
o It was situated to **the south of the river and not near it.** Rajagriha was surrounded by five hills and proved to be impregnable. It not only enjoyed a strategic location, but also lay in the vicinity of iron-encrusted outcrops.
● Later, in the fourth century BCE, the capital was shifted to **Pataliputra,** present-day Patna, commanding routes of communication along the **Ganga**.
o The capital of Magadha did, however, **shift to Pataliputra (originally**
**Pataligramma)** situated on the confluence of several rivers like the Ganga, Gandak, Son and Poon Pun. The rivers could be used as **communication** **routes** by the army moving in the direction of north, west, south and east. Besides, being surrounded by rivers made its position impregnable, functioning as a veritable **water fort (jaladurga).**
● **Pataliputra became the capital of Magadha under the Mauryas**. This enabled Magadha to effectively command the **Uttarapatha (northern route)** which lay to the north of the **river Ganges,** along the foothills of the Himalayas. The river also came to be used as one of the main arteries connecting Magadha with different regions and making heavy transport along the river possible.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with with regard
to Ashokan inscriptions:
I. 	 They were mostly in the Pali language.
II. 	 Most Prakrit inscriptions were written in the
Brahmi script.
III. 	The Aramaic and Greek scripts were used
for inscriptions in the modern day region of
Afghanistan.
How many of the statements given above are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ashoka (273 – 232 B.C.)** was the **first ruler** who inscribed his messages to his subjects and officials on **stone surfaces – natural rocks as well as polished pillars.** He used the inscriptions to proclaim what he understood to be **dhamma**.
● This included respect towards elders, generosity towards Brahmanas and those who renounced worldly life, treating slaves and servants kindly, and respect for religions and traditions other than one’s own.
● Most Ashokan inscriptions were in the **Prakrit language** while those in the northwest of the subcontinent were in Aramaic and Greek. **So, statement I is not correct.**
o Most Prakrit inscriptions were written in the **Brahmi script;** however, some, in the northwest, were written in **Kharosthi. So, statement II is correct.** o The **Aramaic and Greek scripts** were used for inscriptions in Afghanistan. **So, statement III is** **correct.** **So, only two of the above statements are correct.**
**Knowledge Box:** **What are the other Ashokan inscriptions about?**
● **14 Major Rock Edicts of Ashoka** are the separate 14 major rock edicts, spanning across almost India. Except for the Kandahar Greek Edict, which is on a stone plaque, all other major rock edicts were written on large rocks.
● In **Major** **Rock Edict IX**, Ashoka **attacked ceremonies** performed after birth, illness, marriage and before setting out for a journey. Ceremonies carried out by mothers and wives are also condemned.
● **Minor Rock Edicts** are the first inscriptions predating the Major Rock Edicts of Ashoka. Kandahar Bilingual rock edict, which is in Greek and Aramaic, is the first known Ashoka inscription.
o These minor edicts appear in Bahapur, Gujjara, Udegolam, Maski, Nittur, Siddapur, Brahmagiri, Jatinga Rameshwara, Rajula Mandagiri, Yerragudi, Sasaram, Bairat, Ahraura etc. o Only four of these places use the name “**Ashoka”** –  **Maski, Brahmagiri,** **Nettur** and **Gujjara**. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

● The **Buddhist literature** **Anguttara Nikaya** lists sixteen Mahajanapadas that flourished around the 6th century BCE. These were powerful kingdoms or republics with defined capitals and often located along major rivers, which supported agriculture, trade, and communication.
● Each **mahajanapada had a capital city**, which was often fortified. Maintaining these fortified cities as well as providing for incipient armies and bureaucracies required resources.
● They were powerful states in ancient India, emerging around the 6th century BCE, primarily located in the **Indo-Gangetic plains and** **northern Deccan.** These states, which included both kingdoms and republics, played a significant role in shaping the political landscape of the time.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Bimbisara
II. 	 Prasenjit
III.	 Kalashoka
IV. 	 Ajatashatru
How many of the above rulers were contemporaries of Gautam Buddha?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Gautama Buddha (567- 487 B.C.) **the founder of Buddhism**, was born in Lumbini Garden near Kapilavastu.
● **Bimbisara** (543—491 BCE) was one of the early kings of the kingdom of Magadha. His expansion of the kingdom, especially his annexation of the kingdom of Anga to the east, is considered to have laid the foundations for the later expansion of the Mauryan empire. He is also known for his cultural achievements and **was a great friend and protector of the Buddha**. Bimbisara built the city of Rajagriha, famous in Buddhist writings. He was succeeded on the throne by his son Ajatashatru. **So, point I is correct.**
● **King Prasenjit (Pasenadi)** (c. 6th century BCE) ruled over the kingdom of **Kosala**, which bordered Magadha and was also a significant center of Buddhist activity. He was the contemporary of Buddha. **So,** **point II is correct.**
● **Kalashoka (395–367 BCE) or Kakavarna** was **not a contemporary** of Gautama Buddha. Kalashoka was a ruler of the **Shishunaga** dynasty, reigning much later than the time of the Buddha who lived in the 6th to 5th centuries BCE. He presided over the **Second Buddhist Council**. **So, point III is not correct.**
● The son of Bimbisara, **Ajatasatru (494 - 462 B.C.)** was the contemporary of Buddha after ascending to the throne of Magadha. **So, point IV is correct.**
o Buddhists and Jains both claim that Ajatasatru was a follower of their religion. But it is generally believed that in the beginning he was a follower of Jainism and subsequently embraced Buddhism. **He is said to have met Gautam Buddha**. This scene is also **depicted in the sculptures of Barhut**. According to the Mahavamsa, he constructed several chaityas and viharas. He was also instrumental in convening the **First Buddhist Council at Rajagriha** soon after the death of the Buddha. **So, only three of the above rulers were the contemporaries of Gautam Buddha.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:** **Who were the other contemporaries of Buddha?**
● A prominent figure in the Sramana tradition**, Purana Kassapa** was a contemporary of Buddha who propounded a form of moral skepticism.
● Founder of the Ajivika school of thought, **Makkhali Gosala** was another contemporary who presented a deterministic view of life.
● A materialist philosopher, **Ajita Kesakambalin** was a contemporary who rejected the concepts of karma and rebirth.
● **Pakuda Kaccayana** was another contemporary teacher who presented a theory of the soul being composed of seven eternal elements.
● A skeptic philosopher who focused on doubt and uncertainty, **Sanjaya** **Belatthaputta** was also a contemporary of the Buddha.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs with reference to
Persian invasions in India:
King Associated Development
I. - Cyrus Captured the Gandhara region
II. - Darius I Made Punjab and Sindh part of the Satrapy
III. - Xerxes Deployed Indian infantry and cavalry to Greece to fight his opponent
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Persian incursion into India, led by the Achaemenid Empire in the **late 6th century BCE,** was spearheaded by rulers like Cyrus the Great and Darius I. Their campaigns targeted the northwestern regions, notably Punjab and Sindh, drawn by both the region's political fragmentation and its considerable wealth.
● **Cyrus (558 – 530 B.C):** o Cyrus the Great was the greatest conqueror of the Achaemenian Empire. He was the first conqueror who led an expedition and entered into India. He **captured the Gandhara region**. All Indian tribes to the west of the Indus River submitted to him and paid tribute. His son Cambyses had no time to pay attention towards India. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Darius I (522 – 486 B.C.):** o Darius I, the grandson of Cyrus, conquered the Indus valley in 518 B.C. and **annexed the Punjab** **and Sindh.** This region became the **20th Satrapy** of his empire. It was the most fertile and populous province of the Achaemenian Empire. Darius sent a naval expedition under Skylas to explore the Indus. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Xerxes (465-456 B.C.):** o Xerxes utilized his Indian province to strengthen his position. He deployed **Indian infantry and** **cavalry to Greece** to fight his opponents. But they retreated after Xerxes faced a defeat in Greece.
o After this failure, the Achaemenians could not follow a forward policy in India. However, the Indian province was still under their control. Darius III enlisted Indian soldiers to fight against Alexander in 330 B.C. It is evident that the control of Persians slackened on the eve of Alexander’s invasion of India. **So, pair III is correctly matched.** **So, all three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to Alexander’s Invasion of India:
I. 	 Alexander controlled the whole territory
from the Indus up to the Beas.
II. 	 Alexander defeated several republics &
kingdoms in his march from Indus to Beas.
III. 	It paved the way for direct contact between
India and Greece.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
In 327 B.C. **Alexander** crossed the Hindu Kush Mountains and spent nearly ten months fighting with the tribes. He crossed the Indus in February 326 B.C. with the help of the bridge of boats.
● After defeating King Porus in the Battle of Hydaspes (Jhelum), Alexander extended his control from the **Indus River up to the Beas River**. **So, statement I is correct.**
o The **Battle of Hydaspes** was fought on the **plains of Karri**. It was a well-contested battle. Although Porus had a strong army, he lost the battle. Alexander was impressed by the courage and heroism of this Indian prince, treated him generously and **reinstated him on his throne**.
● Alexander continued his march as far as the **river Beas encountering opposition from the local tribes.** He wanted to proceed still further eastwards towards the Gangetic valley. But he could not do so because his **soldiers refused to fight**. **While Alexander did extend his control up to the Beas, he did not cross** **it.** Hardships of prolonged warfare made them tired and they wanted to return home. Alexander could not persuade them and therefore **decided to return**. **So, statement II is correct.**
o He made arrangements to look after his conquered territories in India. He divided the whole territory from the Indus to the Beas into three provinces and put them under his **governors (Greek** **Satraps).** o His retreat began in October 326 B.C. and the return journey was not free from ordeals. Many republican tribes attacked his army. Anyhow he managed to reach beyond the Indus. On his way he reached Babylon where he fell seriously ill and died in 323 B.C.
● The immediate effect of Alexander’s invasion was that it encouraged **political unification of north India** under the Mauryas. The system of small independent states came to an end. Alexander’s invasion had also paved the way for **direct contact between India and Greece**. The routes opened by him and his naval explorations increased the existing facilities for **trade between India and West Asia.** **So, statements II** **and III are correct.** **So, all three of the above statements are correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the coinage in ancient India:
I. 	 Punch-marked coins were among the earliest
coins to be minted.
II. 	 The first coins to bear the names and images
of rulers were issued by the Mauryas.
III. 	The first ever gold coins in India were issued
by the Guptas.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Punch-marked coins** are pieces of metal, typically silver, but sometimes copper, stamped with symbols using a punch. They have distinct features such as irregular shapes and multiple punch marks.
● To some extent, exchanges were facilitated by the introduction of coinage. **Punch-marked coins** made of **silver and copper** (c. sixth century BCE onwards) were **amongst the earliest to be minted and used.** These have been recovered from excavations at a number of sites throughout the subcontinent. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
o Attempts made to identify the symbols on punch marked coins with specific ruling dynasties, including the Mauryas, suggest that these were issued by kings. It is also likely that **merchants,** **bankers and townspeople issued some of these coins.**
● The first coins to bear the names and images of rulers were issued by the **Indo-Greeks**, who established control over the north-western part of the subcontinent c. second century BCE. **So, statement II is not** **correct.**
● **Kushan kings** (first to the third centuries CE) introduced gold and copper coins; a large number of them have survived till today. It was the Kushan emperor, **Vima Kadaphises** who introduced the first gold coins of India**. So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Who issued gold coins in India?**
● The **Kushanas** issued the largest hoards of gold coins. These were **virtually** **identical in weight** with those issued by contemporary Roman emperors and the Parthian rulers of Iran, and have been found from several sites in north India and Central Asia.
o The widespread use of gold coins indicates the enormous value of the
transactions that were taking place. Besides, hoards of Roman coins have been found from archaeological sites in **south India**.
● Coins were also issued by **tribal republics** such as that of the **Yaudheyas** of Punjab and Haryana (c. first century CE).
● Some of the most spectacular gold coins were issued by the **Gupta** rulers. **The** **earliest issues are remarkable for their purity**. These coins facilitated longdistance transactions from which kings also benefited. **Relevance**: Rajasthan’s archaeological sites have recently uncovered a wealth of punch-mark coins dating from 600 BC to 1000 BC.

questions.questionText:
With reference to ancient Indian society, consider
the following pairs:
Term Associated with
I. - Pativedaka Reporter
II. - Sovanika Goldsmith
III. - Nishada Money lender
IV. - Kutagarashala Teacher
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Pativedaka** in the Mauryan administration was an official in ancient Indian administration, whose term has been translated by epigraphists as **reporter**. Their primary function was to **report to the king or ruler** about the affairs of the people at all times and in all places. They provided **regular reports** about happenings in the kingdom, ensuring the ruler was always informed, whether the ruler was eating, resting, travelling, or in private chambers. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Sovanika** is a term found in ancient Indian inscriptions, such as those from Mathura. The term sovanika refers to a **goldsmith**.  **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Nishadas** were **forest-dwelling communities** frequently mentioned in ancient Indian texts, including the **Mahabharata**. They were typically **hunter-gatherers**, collecting wood, honey, tubers, and roots from forests for sustenance and livelihood. Nishadas often lived on the **margins of settled society**, maintaining distinct cultural and economic practices. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
● **Kutagarashala** literally means a **hut with a pointed roof**. In ancient India, especially during the time of the **Buddha**, it referred to a **venue or hall where philosophical debates and discussions were held**. Kutagarashalas were often simple structures located in groves or near monasteries where **travelling** **teachers, philosophers, and mendicants** would halt and engage in intellectual exchanges. **So, pair IV is** **not correctly matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Kalsi rock edict is the only Ashoka’s
Rock edict located in North India.
II. 	 The Girnar inscription in Gujarat records
the details about maintenance of Sudarshana Lake by Rudradaman of Shaka dynasty.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Kalsi** is a small town located in Uttrakhand. It is known for its heritage importance. Famous Mauryan Emperor, Ashoka’s inscriptions as rock edicts are present in Kalsi. **Its importance rises as it is the only** **Ashoka’s Rock edict located in North India**. The language used in these edicts is Pali. Rock edict of Kalsi which depicts the importance of this site along an ancient trade/communication route in the empire. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **Girnar Inscription** is a rock inscription located at Girnar in Gujarat. It primarily records details about the **Sudarshana Lake**, an artificial reservoir, and its maintenance over time.
○ The inscription is in **Sanskrit** and dates to the **second century CE**. The **Sudarshana Lake** was constructed with embankments and water channels by a local governor during the **Mauryan** **period**. ○ The inscription documents that the lake’s embankments were destroyed by a terrible storm, causing severe damage. ○ **Rudradaman**, a Shaka (Western Kshatrapa) ruler, **repaired the lake at his own expense, without** **imposing extra taxes on the people**. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**What is so special about the Mandasor inscription?**
● The **Mandasor inscription** dates to the **5th century CE** and was found in Mandasor (Dashapura), Madhya Pradesh. It records the **history of a guild (shreni) of silk weavers.** The inscription states that the silk weavers originally lived in **Lata (Gujarat)** and migrated to Mandasor with their families and kin, inspired by the fame of the local king and his kingdom.
○ The document highlights the existence of **well-organized guilds (shrenis)**, with membership primarily based on craft specialization (silk weaving). The guild members collectively invested their wealth (earned through their craft) to construct a temple in honor of the sun god.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Literature of Ancient India
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Buddhist Text Theme
I. - Matanga Jataka
Illustrates the virtue of dana (generosity) and selflessness
II. - Majjhima Nikaya
Collection of middlelength discourses
III. - Vessantara Jataka
Recounts the previous births of the Buddha
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Matanga Jataka** is a story from the **Jataka tales** (Pali Buddhist literature), which recounts the previous births of the Buddha (Bodhisatta). In the **Matanga Jataka**, the Buddha is born as **Matanga**, a **chandala’s son**. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
○ When Matanga enters **Benaras**, Dittha-Mangalika, a merchant’s daughter, publicly declares his presence as inauspicious. The crowd then **beats Matanga unconscious** due to his low social status.
● **Majjhima Nikaya** is one of the five **Nikayas (collections)** of the **Sutta Pitaka**, which forms part of the **Pali Canon** in Theravada Buddhism. The term “Majjhima Nikaya” means **“Collection of Middle-Length** **Discourses”**, as it contains suttas (discourses) of moderate length. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
○ The text reflects **Buddhist views on social equality** and critiques rigid social hierarchies like the varna (caste) system. The story of the **wealthy Shudra** (from Majjhima Nikaya) demonstrates that **wealth, not birth, determines actual social power**, challenging Brahmanical claims of superiority. ○ The **Majjhima Nikaya** contains **152 suttas** attributed to the Buddha and his close disciples. These discourses cover a wide range of topics like Ethics and morality, Meditation and mental training, Social issues and caste, Philosophy and the nature of reality, etc.
● The **Vessantara Jataka** is a well-known Buddhist tale about **Prince Vessantara**, celebrated for his **extraordinary generosity**. In the story, Vessantara gives away all his possessions, including his prized elephant, and finally even his wife and children to a Brahmana. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
○ After losing everything, he goes to live in the **forest** with his family. The story is meant to illustrate the Buddhist virtue of **dana (generosity)** and selflessness. ○ Scenes from the Vessantara Jataka are depicted in ancient **Buddhist art**, such as at **Sanchi**, where sculptors represented moments from the story in stone to inspire moral reflection and devotion among viewers.
● The Sutta Pitaka, the second division of the Tipitaka, consists of more than 10,000 suttas (discourses) delivered by the Buddha and his close disciples. The **Sutta Pitaka** contains an excerpt describing a conversation between king Ajatasattu, the ruler of Magadha, and the Buddha. **So, only one of the pairs given above is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The Iron Age in India contributed to the rise of Mahajanapadas.
**Statement II:** The use of iron tools led to increased agricultural productivity and population growth.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Janapada**, literally meaning the place where the people place their feet. In the later Vedic phase the members of Jana took up agriculture and began to settle down. These agricultural settlements were called **Janapadas**. In the initial phases these settlements were named after the dominant Kshatriya lineages settled in that area. For example, the areas around Delhi and upper U.P. were known as the Kuru and Panchal Janapadas after the names of the dominant Kshatriya lineages. **Once they settled down in one** **place there was all expansion of agriculture, especially with the use of iron axes and plough shares**. These **iron tools could clear the forests and dig deeper than the stone or copper tools** available to the
agriculturists df the preceding centuries. The middle Gangetic Valley i.e. the area east of Allahabad was suited to wet rice cultivation. Rice yields are higher than wheat yields per acre. All this would lead to gradual agricultural expansion as also growth of population. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Some of the Janapadas developed into Mahajanapadas by the sixth century B.C**. This happened as a result of a series of changes in the internal socio-political structure of the Janapadas. **One such important** **change was the expansion of agricultural communities.** **So, statement I is correct.**
● By about 1000 B.C. Indians had learnt the art of smelting iron. Over the next three or four centuries iron came into increasing use. That is why **a large number of iron tools and implements have been found** **from Ujjain, Sravasti and Hastinapur**. Iron weapons in particular have been found in large numbers. These would have **increased the power of the warrior classes vis-a-vis the peasants**. With more powerful weapons the warrior classes could extract more wealth from the peasants. **Iron weapons also** **increased their desire for war, conquest and booty.** **So,  both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
With reference to Buddhist philosophy, the terms
‘anicca’ and ‘anatta’ refer to:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Buddha’s teachings**, preserved mainly in the **Sutta Pitaka**, emphasize reason and persuasion over miracles.
● Stories often show the Buddha gently guiding people to accept truths like the **inevitability of death**, using simple language for ordinary people.
● According to Buddhist philosophy, the world is **transient (anicca)**, **soulless (anatta)**, and **full of** **suffering (dukkha)**. The path to overcoming suffering lies in **moderation**, avoiding both extreme selfdenial and indulgence.
○ **Anicca** means **impermanence,** the Buddhist idea that everything in the world is transient and constantly changing. Nothing lasts forever. ○ **Anatta** means **non-self or soullessness,** the belief that there is no permanent, unchanging soul or self within beings. This principle explains a continuity of mental and physical processes that gives rise to the sense of self. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**The doctrine of paticca-samuppada**
● **Paticca-samuppada,** the chain, or law, of dependent origination, or the c**hain of** **causation**—a fundamental concept of Buddhism describing the causes of suffering (dukkha; Sanskrit duhkha) and the course of events that lead a being through rebirth, old age, and death.
● Existence is seen as **an interrelated flux of phenomenal events**, material and psychical, without any real, permanent, independent existence of their own. These events happen in a series, one interrelating group of events producing another. The series is usually described as a chain of 12 links (nidanas, “causes”), though some texts abridge these to 10, 9, 5, or 3.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
“Be lamps unto yourselves as all of you must work
out your own liberation” reflects a fundamental teaching of which one of the following personalities?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Gautama Buddha** taught that the social world is shaped by **human actions**, not divine will. He urged kings and householders (gahapatis) to act humanely and ethically.
● He stressed individual effort and righteous action as the path to end suffering and achieve nibbana (liberation), meaning the end of ego and desire.
● His message encouraged self-reliance was **“Be lamps unto yourselves, as all of you must work out your** **own liberation”.** It emphasizes individual responsibility for spiritual development and liberation from suffering. This means relying on one's own inner wisdom and effort rather than external sources for enlightenment. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**What were the key teachings of Gautama Buddha?**
● **Core Teachings** of the Buddha focus on practical solutions to human suffering, as reconstructed from the **Sutta Pitaka**. The Buddha emphasized the **Four Noble Truths** **(Chatwari Arya Satyani):**
○ Sabbam Dukkam: The world is full of suffering. ○ Dwadash Nidan: Suffering is caused by desire, ignorance, and attachment ○ Nirvana: Suffering can end by destroying its cause. ○ Ashtangika Marga: The way to end suffering is the Noble Eightfold Path.
● The **Eightfold Path** includes: ○ Right view ○ Right aim ○ Right speech ○ Right action ○ Right livelihood ○ Right effort ○ Right mindfulness ○ Right concentration Following this path leads to liberation from suffering, without reliance on priests or rituals, reflecting the Buddha's practical and ethical approach to spiritual progress.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Buddhist sangha:
I. 	 Mahapajapati Gotami was the first woman
member of the sangha.
II. 	 Women teachers of the sangha were called as
theris.
III. 	People had to leave behind their social
identities to get admission into the Sangha.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The word **“Sangha”** is a **Sanskrit** term meaning **“community”**. It primarily refers to the Buddha’s ordained followers, called **bhikkhus** (monks) and **bhikshunis** (nuns).
● The **Buddha** founded a **sangha**, an organisation of monks called **bhikkhus** who lived simply, relying on alms for survival.
● Initially, only men were admitted, but later, through the efforts of Ananda, women were also allowed; the Buddha’s foster mother, **Mahapajapati Gotami**, became the first **bhikkhuni**. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ **Mahapajapati Gotami** was the **Buddha’s foster mother** and **maternal aunt**, married to King Suddhodana after Queen Maha Maya’s death.
● The sangha included followers from all social backgrounds, kings, wealthy men, workers, slaves, and craftspeople, who became equals within the community. **Many women in the sangha became respected** **teachers called** **theris**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The sangha operated on principles of discussion, consensus, and voting, reflecting traditions of earlier ganas and sanghas, and **all members left behind their former social identities**. **So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History

questions.questionText:
With reference to the Buddhist stupas, consider
the following statements:
I. 	 The tradition of erecting stupas is pre-
Buddhist in origin.
II. 	 The Ashokavadana text recounts the construction of several stupas by Emperor Ashoka.
III. 	The Shahji-ki-Dheri stupa was constructed
by Ashoka in modern Peshawar.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **stupa** is a **dome-shaped sacred monument** that contains relics of the Buddha or other sacred objects, holding great religious and symbolic importance in Buddhist architecture.
○ Stupas originated in India before the 5th century BCE, evolving from simple burial mounds into elaborate monuments central to Buddhist worship. ○ The term appears in the Rigveda (estuka) and Atharvaveda, while the Pali word “thupa” refers to a shrine or relic mound.
● The tradition of building **stupas** may have **existed before Buddhism**, but it became closely linked with Buddhism because stupas housed sacred relics of the Buddha. As a result, stupas were venerated as symbols of the Buddha and his teachings. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ According to scholars this kind of mound is not uncommon since prehistory period; this idea developed based on own faith and culture as in the case of Buddhist Stupa. In ancient times, the stupa or mound was crude form. But later it developed from the simple structure of the mound to highly sophisticated for particularly, found in the Buddhist tradition.
● According to the **Ashokavadana**, **Emperor Asoka** distributed Buddha s relics to various towns and ordered stupas to be constructed over them. **So, statement II is correct.**
● By the **2nd century BCE**, many stupas such as those at **Bharhut, Sanchi, and Sarnath** had been built.
● **Shah-ji-ki Dheri stupa** is the site of the famous **Kanishka Stupa**, built by the Kushana ruler **Kanishka** **I** in **Purushapura** (modern Peshawar). **So, statement III is not correct.**
○ This stupa, constructed in the **first centuries AD**, was renowned as the most significant stupa monument of its time in the Indian subcontinent. It featured an **elongated “tower stupa” design** standing on a **cruciform platform**. ○ According to the Chinese pilgrim **Hui-sheng** (518–522 CE), the Kanishka Stupa was considered the **foremost temple in the western regions**.
● Early stupas were simple hemispherical mounds of earth or brick, but later developed intricate carvings, reliefs, and sculptures, reflecting the growth of Buddhism and its architectural traditions. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
The “Elliot Marbles” are associated with which of
the following ancient Indian sculptures?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Amaravati sculptures**, also called **Eliot Marbles**, are a collection of nearly 120 exquisite Buddhist sculptures and inscriptions from the ancient Amaravati site near Guntur, Andhra Pradesh.
● Sir Walter Elliot was an accomplished Orientalist and civil servant, whose work in Guntur led to the excavation and transfer of the Amaravati sculptures, hence the name **Eliot Marbles** (though the pieces are actually limestone, not marble).
● The Amaravati pieces include toranams (gateways), pillars, drum panels, guardian lions, and scenes from Buddhist Jataka tales, reflecting the rich artistic and religious heritage of Amaravati.
● These sculptures are now displayed in the British Museum, London, and are known as Eliot Marbles due to their association with **Sir Walter Elliot,** who excavated them in the 1840s.
● Initial explorations were conducted by **Colin Mackenzie** in 1798, but major excavations took place under **Walter Elliot** in 1845. The sculptures were first displayed in Madras and later recommended by **Edward** **Belfour** for transfer to England for better preservation. By 1859, the Court of Directors ordered their transportation to London. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
The motifs ‘Shalabhanjika’, ‘Gajalakshmi’  and
‘Maya Devi’ are associated with:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Motifs at Sanchi Stupa:**
● **Shalabhanjika** motif suggests that many people who turned to Buddhism enriched it with their own preBuddhist and even non-Buddhist beliefs, practices and ideas.
○ This was a woman whose touch caused trees to flower and bear fruit. ○ It was **not directly inspired** by Buddhist ideas.
● **The Gajalakshmi motif** is another prominent feature, showing the goddess Lakshmi seated on a lotus with **elephants (gaja)** **pouring water over her** (abhisheka).
○ This iconography symbolizes **wealth, prosperity, and** **good fortune**. Gajalakshmi was popular in Buddhist, Jain, and Brahmanical art, and her worship is connected to ritual bathing (abhisheka) practices.
● At **Sanchi**, the **dream of Maya Devi** is depicted in high relief from the **first century BCE**. Maya Devi is shown reclining on a decorated couch, with emphasis on the intricate decoration rather than the attendants. A **peacock** sits on the roof, enhancing the scene's beauty. The **Bodhisatta** appears near her feet, representing the moment he enters her dream. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Painted Grey Ware period in ancient India:
I. 	 It belongs to the Iron age of North India.
II. 	 The sites of this culture are located along
river-banks.
III. 	The upper Ganges-Jamuna valley witnessed
a large-scale cultivation in this period.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Painted Grey Ware was a special type of pottery used during the Later Vedic Period**.. The **Iron Age** **of north India** is broadly associated with PGW culture. The PGW was an iron-using rural culture. A large number of sites belonging to the Later Vedic Period have been excavated. They are all marked by a typical pottery called Painted Grey Ware (PGW), and so are known as PGW sites. Broadly the time period of PGW culture is between 1000-600 BCE. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The sites of this culture are **located along river-banks**, the average distance from one site to another being about 10 to 12 km. However, in a favourable ecological environment it could be as little as 5 km. These settlements were mostly small villages. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **credit of bringing the upper Ganges-Jamuna valley under large-scale cultivation goes to the** **Painted Grey Ware people**. They could achieve this mainly because of their iron technology to which a detailed reference will be made later. They cleared the heavy jungle with the help of iron axes. Of the ploughshare no example has so far been found from this period, though the subsequent period has yielded examples. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● Painted Grey Ware had painted designs, usually simple lines and geometric patterns. **Plates and bowls were the most common vessels made out of Painted Grey Ware**. These are extremely fine to touch, with a nice, smooth surface.
● Perhaps these were **used on special occasions**, for important people, and to serve special food.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Rig Vedic age:
I. 	 Jana was the chief social unit.
II. 	 It had a regular standing army and a complex
taxation system.
III. 	Rajan enjoyed a hereditary post.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Early Vedic period** is known mainly from the Rig Veda, which was the first Veda to be composed. For this period (1500-1000BC), the Vedic tribes lived in the northwest part of the subcontinent. In the 6th century BC there came up 16 large territorial states in northern and eastern India known as the Mahajanapadas.
● The **chief social unit of the Rigvedic Aryans was known as jana**. The chief of this unit was the political leader called rajan. In Rigvedic age, the sons did not automatically succeed fathers as rajas. **The post of** **the raja was not hereditary.** The tribe generally elected him. **So, statement I is correct and statement** **III is not correct.**
● The **Rigvedic Rajas did not have capital cities, palaces or armies, nor did they collect taxes.** Generally, sons did not automatically succeed fathers as rajas. Some of the rajas were probably chosen by the jana, the people. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy

questions.questionText:
Which of the following changes in agriculture were
introduced during the period of Mahajanapadas?
I. 	 Use of iron plough-share
II. 	 Transplantation method in paddy cultivation
III. 	Cultivation of millets and sugarcane
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In the 6th century BC there came up 16 large territorial states in northern and eastern India known as the Mahajanapadas.
● There were two major changes in agriculture during the period of Mahajanapadas. One was the **growing** **use of iron ploughshares.** This meant that heavy, clayey soil could be turned over better than with a wooden ploughshare, so that more grain could be produced.
○ The **use of iron ploughshare began during the Later Vedic Period** (1000 BCE to 600 BCE). Its use became widespread during the period of Mahajanapadas. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Second, people began transplanting paddy**. This meant that instead of scattering seed on the ground, from which plants would sprout, saplings were grown and then planted in the fields. This led to increased production, as many more plants survived. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Millet, and sugarcane were cultivated even during the Later Vedic Age (1000 BCE to 600 BCE). So,** **statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct
with reference to the schools of Indian Philosophy?
I. 	 Samkhya considers self-knowledge as an
ultimate path to gain salvation.
II. 	 Nyaya holds the view that knowledge can be
acquired only through perceptions.
III. 	Vaisheshika does not believe in the law of
karma.
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● There are six systems of philosophy in India. These are known as Vaishesika, Nyaya, Samkhya, Yoga, Purva Mimansa and Vedanta or Uttara Mimansa.
● The Samkhya philosophy **holds that reality is constituted of two principles one female and the other** **male i.e. Prakriti and Purusha respectively.** Prakriti and Purusha are completely independent and absolute.
○ According to this system, Purusha is mere consciousness, hence it cannot be modified or changed. Prakriti on the other hand is constituted of three attributes, thought, movement and the change or transformation of these attributes brings about the change in all objects. ○ The Samkhya philosophy tries to establish some relationship between Purusha and Prakriti for explaining the creation of the universe. The propounder of this philosophy was Kapila, who wrote the Samkhya sutra. It argued that **salvation could be attained through the acquisition of** **knowledge. So, statement I is correct.**
● **Nyaya is considered as a technique of logical thinking**. According to Nyaya, valid **knowledge is** **defined as real knowledge, that is, one knows about the object** as it exists. It is a system of atomistic pluralism and logical realism.
○ It **believes in the external things as reflecting their real nature when knowledge is true**, and their unreal nature when knowledge is false. Consequently, for them, knowledge is the knowledge of things, and it constitutes the expression of reality. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Vaisheshika system** is considered as the realistic and objective philosophy of the universe. The reality according to this philosophy has many bases or categories which are substance, attribute, action, genus, distinct quality and inherence. Vaisheshika thinkers believe that all objects of the universe are composed of five elements–**earth, water, air, fire and ether**. They believe that **God is the guiding principle**.
○ **They believe that the laws of karma guide this Universe,** i.e. everything is based on the actions of the human beings. We are rewarded or punished according to our actions. **So, statement III is** **not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Schools of Indian Philosophy:**
● **Yoga** School postulates that one can achieve salvation by combining meditation and physical application.
● **Mimamsa** philosophy of Jamini is the analysis of interpretation, application and use of the text of the Samhita and Brahmana portions of the Veda.
● **Vedanta** School of Vyasa states that Brahm is the reality of life, and everything else is unreal. They equalise atma and Brahm, and if a person attains the knowledge of the self, he would automatically understand Brahm and achieve salvation.

Consider the following statements with regard to
the Grama Bhojaka in ancient India:
I. 	 He performed both the revenue and judicial
functions.
II. 	 His post was usually hereditary.
III. 	He was often the largest landowner of the
village.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In Ancient India, the village headman was known as the grama bhojaka.  Usually, men from the same family held the position for generations. In other words, **the post was hereditary. So, statement II is** **correct.**
● The grama bhojaka was often the **largest landowner**. Generally, he had slaves and hired workers to cultivate the land. Besides, as he was powerful, **the king often used him to collect taxes from the village.** **He also functioned as a judge**, and sometimes as a policeman.  **So, statements I and III are correct.** **So, all three of the above statements are correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
It narrates the overthrow of the Nanda dynasty by Chandragupta Maurya with the assistance of Kautilya, and also provides valuable insights into the socio-economic conditions of the Mauryan period’.
Which is this text?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Literary developments in the Gupta period:** ○ **Mudrarakshasa:** It is written by **Visakadatta** and is a drama in **Sanskrit**. Although written during the **Gupta period**, it describes how **Chandragupta** with the assistance of Kautilya **overthrew the** **Nandas**. It also gives a picture on the socio-economic condition under the Mauryas. ○ **Kritarjuniya**: It is written by **Bharavi and** is the story of the conflict between Arjuna and Shiva.
● **Literary developments associated with the Mauryan period:** ○ **Indica:** It was written by **Megasthenes**. Megasthenes was the **Greek ambassador** in the court of **Chandragupta Maurya.** His book Indica has survived only in fragments. Yet, his account gives details about the Mauryan administration, particularly the administration of the capital city of **Pataliputra** and also the military organization.
■ His picture on contemporary social life is notable. Certain unbelievable information provided by him has to be treated with caution. ○ **Arthasastra:** It was written by **Kautilya** in Sanskrit, a contemporary of Chandragupta Maurya. Kautilya was also called ‘**Indian Machiavelli’**. The manuscript of Arthasastra was first discovered by **R. Shama Sastri** in 1904.
■ The Arthasastra contains 15 books and 180 chapters but it can be divided into three parts: the first deals with the king and his council and the departments of government; the second with civil and criminal law; and the third with diplomacy and war. ■ It is the most important literary source for the history of the Mauryas. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:** **Other notable works:**
● **Sudraka** was a renowned poet of this age and his book **Mrichchakatika** is rich in humour and pathos.
● **Devichandraguptam**: It was  a Sanskrit drama written by **Visakadatta**. It is known for its dramatic narrative about the Gupta dynasty and specifically the rise of **Chandragupta II.**
● **Dandin** was the author of **Kavyadarsa** and **Dasakumaracharita**.
● Another important work of this period was **Vasavadatta** written by **Subhandhu**.
● The **Panchatantra stories** were composed by **Vishnusarma** during the Gupta period.
● The Buddhist author **Amarasimha** compiled a lexicon called **Amarakosa**.

questions.questionText:
In the Mauryan empire, the post of ‘Gopa’ was
primarily associated with which one of the following?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Mauryan Empire was divided into four provinces with their capitals at Taxila, Ujjain,** **Suvarnagiri and Kalinga**. The provincial governors were mostly appointed from the members of the royal family. They were responsible for the maintenance of law and order and collection of taxes for the empire.
● The **district administration was under the charge of** **Rajukas**, whose position and functions are similar to modern collectors. He was assisted by Yuktas or subordinate officials. Village administration was in the hands of Gramani and his official superior was called **Gopa who was in charge of ten or fifteen** **villages.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Sungas:
I. 	 Agnimitra was the founder of the Sunga
dynasty.
II. 	 Pushyamitra renovated Buddhist monuments
at Bharhut.
III. 	Devabhuti fought a campaign against
Kharavela of Kalinga who invaded north India.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The founder of the **Sunga dynasty** (185 BC – 73 BC)was **Pushyamitra Sunga,** who was the **commander-** **in-chief under the Mauryas.** He assassinated the last Mauryan ruler and usurped the throne. **So, statement** **I is not correct.**
● After the death of Pushyamitra, **his son Agnimitra** became the ruler. The last Sunga ruler was **Devabhuti,** **who was murdered by his minister Vasudeva Kanva**, the founder of the Kanva dynasty. The Kanva dynasty ruled for 45 years. After the fall of the Kanvas, the history of Magadha was a blank until the establishment of the Gupta dynasty. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● Buddhist sources refer Pushyamitra as a **persecutor of Buddhism**. But there is enough evidence to show that Pushyamitra **patronised Buddhist art**. During his reign the Buddhist monuments at **Bharhut and Sanchi** were renovated and further improved. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The most important challenge to the Sunga rule was to protect north India against the invasions of the Bactrian Greeks from the northwest. The Greeks advanced up to Pataliputra and occupied it for some time. However, Pushyamitra succeeded in **regaining the lost territory**. He also fought a **campaign against Kharavela of** **Kalinga** who invaded north India. **So, only one of the above statements is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History

questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Personality Associated development
I. - Nagarjuna Composed Milindapanha
II. - Heliodorus Erected the Besnagar Garuda Pillar
III. - Appolodotus Issued bilingual coins
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Bactria and Parthia** became independent from the Syrian empire in the middle of the third century B.C. Demetrius, the Greek ruler of Bactria invaded Afghanistan and Punjab and occupied them. From Taxila, he sent two of his commanders, Appolodotus and Menander for further conquests.
● **Appolodotus conquered Sindh** and marched up to Ujjain. Menander extended his rule up to Mathura and from there he made attempts to capture Pataliputra. But he was stopped by the army of Vasumitra, the grandson of Pushyamitra Sunga.
● **Menander** was also known as **Milinda** and the capital of his kingdom was **Sakala (Sialcot).** He evinced much interest in Buddhism and his dialogues with the Buddhist monk **Nagasena (Nagarjuna)** was compiled in the Pali work, **Milindapanho (Questions of Milinda).** He also embraced Buddhism. **So, pair I is correctly** **matched.**
● A Greek ambassador **Heliodorus** became a Vaishnavite and erected the **Garuda Pillar** at Besnagar. **So, pair** **II is correctly matched.**
● **Appolodotus** was an Indo-Greek ruler; he ruled western and southern part of the Indo-Greek kingdom of India between 180 BCE and 160 BCE or between 170 BCE and 175 BCE. **Appolodotus issued a large** **number of bilingual coins in India**. He also issued **coins depicting animals**. One side of this depicts **Greek** **legend** and the other side of this coin depicts **Kharoshti script. So, pair III is correctly matched.** **So, all three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Subject**: History | Post Mauryan Age **Tags**: Factual
