questions.questionText:
Which of the following rulers were associated
with the construction of the Qutub Minar?
I. - Qutbuddin Aibak
II. 	 Iltutmish
III. 	Alauddin Khilji
IV. 	 Firoz Shah Tughlaq
V. 	 Sikandar Lodi
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:

options.b: II, III, IV and V only
options.d: I, II, III, IV and V

questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Qutub Minar** is an iconic monument in **Delhi**, built as a victory tower to mark the beginning of Muslim rule in India in the 13th century.
● The construction of Qutub Minar was **started by Qutbuddin Aibak** and **completed by Iltutmish**. This seventy one metre tower was **dedicated to** **the Sufi saint Qutbuddin Bakthiyar Kaki**. The balconies of this tower were projected from the main building and it was proof of the architectural skills of that period. Later, **Alauddin Khalji added an** **entrance to the Qutub Minar called Alai Darwaza**. The dome of this arch was built on scientific lines. **So, points I, II and III are correct.**
● Over the years it was repaired over the years by many rulers, such as by **Firoz Shah Tughlaq** and **Sikander Lodhi** after it was damaged. **So,** **points IV and V are correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Which of the following best describes the ‘Turkan-
i-Chahalgani’ system in the context of medieval
Indian history?

options.a:
A group of nobles formed by Iltutmish for
administrative and military support
options.b:
A revenue assessment method introduced by
Alauddin Khalji
options.c:
A cavalry ranking system introduced during
Akbar’s reign
options.d:
A code of law enforced by Balban for judicial
reforms

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Turkan-i-Chahalgani** (also called the bandagan Turk Chihilgan or group of ‘forty’ Turkish slave officers) was a powerful **administrative and military institution** created by **Iltutmish**, the Sultan of Delhi. This group consisted mainly of elite Turkish slaves who had risen to positions of high authority and trust.
● The group was made up of **Turkish slaves** who excelled in military and administrative abilities. These slaves were carefully selected and trained, often given high positions such as governors (iqtadars) and military commanders.
● They formed the backbone of the Sultanate’s administration and military, ensuring stability and the consolidation of Iltutmish's rule. The system was characterized by a high degree of **loyalty**, **unflinching** **courage**, **commitment**, and **administrative skill**.
● Members of the Chahalgani were provided with **land grants (iqtas)**, military command, and crucial roles in governance. Some prominent members included **Malik Izzuddin Kabir Khan Ayaz**, **Malik Saifuddin** **Kuchik**, and **Malik Altunia**. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
With reference to medieval India, consider the
following pairs:
Ruler Coin introduced
I. - Iltutmish Copper Jital
II. - Sher Shah Suri Silver Rupiya
III. - Sikandar Lodi Silver Tanka
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Iltutmish (1210-36)** is credited with **standardizing the coinage** of the **Delhi Sultanate**, creating a currency system that persisted throughout the Sultanate period. He introduced **gold and silver tankas** as well as the **copper jital**, with the jital valued at 1/48th of a tanka in North India and 1/50th in the Deccan after the conquest of Devagiri. A fixed ratio of 1:10 between gold and silver was established. **So, pair I** **is correctly matched.**
● During his brief reign (**1538–1540 CE**), **Sher Shah Suri** established a new currency system in India, replacing the earlier billon coins with a tri-metallic system consisting of gold, silver, and copper coins. He introduced the **silver Rupiya,** which became the precursor to the modern rupee, and **copper coins called** **Paisa**, each weighing around **20 grams**. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● The **Lodi dynasty**  **(1451–1526)** coinage system is marked by a **scarcity of gold and silver coins** and a dominance of **billon and copper coins** in various proportions throughout the successive reigns of the Lodi emperors. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Iqta system in medieval
India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 It was institutionalised by Iltutmish.
II. 	 The Iqtadar held similar powers in his
province as the Sultan had in the empire.
III. 	It was made hereditary by Firuz Shah
Tughlaq.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Iqta system was an administrative regional unit**, considered similar to a province, introduced by the Delhi Sultans. The empire was divided into several Iqtas or provinces, each placed under the charge of an officer called the Iqtadar (governor).
● The **Iltutmish** **institutionalized the Iqta system** by dividing the Sultanate into several Iqtas. He appointed **Muqtis** as administrators of these units, who were responsible for governing their respective regions, collecting taxes, and maintaining law and order.  **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Iqtadar operated under the supervision of the **Central government** and carried out the **orders of the** **Sultan**. He held **similar powers in his province as the Sultan had in the empire.** The Iqtadar maintained large armies, sent troops to the Sultan when required, collected **revenue**, and protected life and property. He appointed soldiers, managed revenue, oversaw expenditures (army, administration), and sent annual reports to the center. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The Iqtadar did **not always enjoy hereditary powers**; the Sultan could take back an Iqta at will. The Iqtadar could be **transferred** and was restricted from engaging in independent wars or using royal privileges (court, canopy, emblem, minting coins, khutba). He was also obliged to send part of the war booty and any captured royals or elephants to the Sultan. Later, **Firuz Shah Tughluq** made Iqtas hereditary and fixed revenue assessments, further weakening central authority.  **So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About Iqta System:**
● The number of Iqtas was **not fixed, and there was no uniformity in their administration**.
● Iqtas were divided into **shiqqs**, **parganas**, and **villages** for administrative efficiency. Officials like **Shiqqdar** (shiqq head), **Amil** or **Munsif** (pargana level), and **Qanungo** (treasurer) assisted governance.
● Under Balban, stricter revenue audits and the role of the khwaja (accountant) were introduced.
● **Alauddin Khilji brought more central control**, created the Khalisa (crown lands) for direct revenue collection, and started paying soldiers in cash.
● **Muhammad bin Tughluq** separated **tax collection** and **troop maintenance**, appointing **Amirs** for military duties and Muqtis for revenue, aiming for more royal income but causing dissatisfaction among commanders.
● Under the Lodhis, administrative and revenue assignments merged into sarkars and parganas.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Iltutmish shifted the capital from Lahore to
Delhi, establishing it as the political center of
the Delhi Sultanate.
II. 	 Ghiyasuddin Balban introduced Sijda
(prostration) and Paibos (kissing the Sultan’s
feet) to reinforce the Sultan’s superiority.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Iltutmish (1211-1236 CE)** was the **third Sultan of Delhi** and the true consolidator of Turkish rule in India, belonging to the Ilbari dynasty. Iltutmish shifted the **capital from Lahore to Delhi**, establishing it as the political center of the **Delhi Sultanate**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Ghiyasuddin Balban (1246-1287)**, also known as Ulugh Khan, first served as Naib (regent) to Sultan Nasiruddin Mahmud and further solidified his influence by marrying his daughter to the Sultan. He introduced rigorous court discipline and ceremonies such as prostration (Sijda) and kissing the Sultan’s feet (Paibos) to reinforce the Sultan’s superiority. He is known to eliminate the **"Corps of Forty"** **(Chahalgani)**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Raziya Sultan (1236–1240 CE) was the first and only woman ruler of the Delhi Sultanate. The **Mongol** **incursions affected the Delhi Sultanate’s territories during the rule of Iltutmish** (between 1221 and 1327). **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following cities was popularly
made the second capital by Mohammad Bin
Tughlaq?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Mohammad Bin Tughlaq transferred his capital from Delhi to Devagiri (renamed Daulatabad) in** **1327 CE with the objective of strengthening administrative control over the Deccan** and safeguarding the empire from Mongol invasions. In a controversial and drastic decision, he forcibly moved the entire population of Delhi to the new capital. However, this policy failed due to lack of adequate water supply in Daulatabad and logistical difficulties.
● The capital was **shifted back to Delhi after two years**. The distance between Delhi and Daulatabad was over 1500 kilometres, and the forced migration during the harsh summer led to the death of many people. This episode is often cited as an example of Mohammad Bin Tughlaq’s **ambitious but impractical** **policies.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Official Function
I. - Amir-i-Tuzuk Head of the military department
II. - Ariz-i-Mumalik Controller of royal ceremonies
III. Diwan-i-Insha Head of the royal correspondence
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Delhi Sultanate established a highly structured administrative system to manage its vast empire. Several specialized officials were appointed to oversee various aspects of governance such as military affairs, royal protocol, and communication.
● **Amir-i-Tuzuk was the officer in charge of court protocol and royal ceremonies.** This official was responsible for regulating the Sultan's public appearances, court etiquette, and ceremonial conduct within the palace. He ensured that all royal customs and rituals were properly observed, especially during formal gatherings and diplomatic receptions. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **Ariz-i-Mumalik served as the head of the military department and was essentially the war minister**. His primary responsibilities included the recruitment, organization, and maintenance of the army. He supervised the registration of soldiers, ensured the supply of arms and horses, and managed the salaries of troops. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● **Diwan-i-Insha was in charge of the Sultanate's official correspondence and record-keeping**. This officer headed the department responsible for drafting royal farmans (orders), letters, and diplomatic communications. The Diwan-i-Insha ensured that all written communication—both internal (to provincial governors, officials) and external (to foreign rulers or ambassadors)—was carried out in accordance with state protocol. **So, pair III is correctly matched.** **So, only one of the pairs given above is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Who were the ‘Barids’ and ‘Munhiyans’ in the
Delhi Sultanate?

options.a:
Land revenue assessors and tax collectors
under the Iqtadari system
options.b:
Spies and intelligence officers to monitor the
administration
options.c:
Village headmen responsible for revenue
collection
options.d:
Scholars associated with the maintenance of
historical records  in royal courts

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **In the Delhi Sultanate, particularly under rulers like Balban and Alauddin Khilji,** **Barids** **and** **Munhiyans** **were intelligence officers or spies appointed by the Sultan**. They were tasked with reporting the activities of nobles, governors, and even members of the royal household. Barids typically operated as informants stationed in provinces.
● **Munhiyans** were mobile secret agents who travelled and provided firsthand reports. This intelligence system was crucial for maintaining central control over a vast and often rebellious empire. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText:
With reference to agrarian reforms during the
Delhi Sultanate period, consider the following
statements:
I. 	 Alauddin Khalji was the first Sultan to fix
land revenue after a detailed land assessment.
II. 	 Mohammad bin Tuhglaq granted Sondhar
loans to support agricultural activities.
III. 	Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq undertook construction
of canals to enhance agricultural productivity.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Delhi Sultanate (1206–1526) introduced several important agrarian reforms that laid the foundation for a more centralized and revenue-oriented administration. These reforms aimed at increasing state income, improving agricultural output, and asserting state control over land and peasants.
● **Alauddin Khalji was the first Sultan of Delhi to implement systematic land measurement as part of** **revenue administration**. He ensured that even influential landlords were not exempt from paying land taxes. The revenue was collected in cash, enabling the Sultan to maintain a salaried army. State officials carried out the measurement of cultivated land, using biswa as the standard unit, and determined the land revenue accordingly. The state demand was fixed at one-fifth of the total produce, and additional levies such as house tax (**Grahi**) and pasture tax (**Chari**) were imposed.  **So, statement I is correct.**
● **The term ‘Sondhar’ refers to agricultural loans extended by the state**, particularly under **Muhammad** **bin Tughlaq** and his successor Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq. These loans were intended to **help peasants during** **natural calamities** or crop failures and were aimed at reviving cultivation and the rural economy. The policy reflected a degree of state responsibility towards agrarian recovery.  **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq took an active interest in improving agriculture and rural infrastructure**. Among his key initiatives were the **construction and maintenance of canals for irrigation**, which were crucial for expanding cultivation, especially in arid regions. He also promoted the reclamation of wastelands and encouraged peasants to bring more land under the plough. These steps reflect a practical and welfare-oriented approach to agrarian administration. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Iltutmish conquered Lahore and Multan as
part of his frontier policy against Mongol threats.
II. 	 Alauddin Khilji fortified key frontier towns
like Bhatinda, Sunam and Samana to resist Mongol invasions.
III. 	Balban built the Siri Fort in Delhi to protect
against Mongol incursions.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Mongol threat to India emerged in the early 13th century following the expansion of Genghis Khan and his successors. The Delhi Sultans had to adopt both defensive and administrative strategies to safeguard the empire’s northwestern frontiers from Mongol invasions. Iltutmish, Balban, and Alauddin Khilji, in particular, played pivotal roles in framing India’s early frontier and fortification policy against the Mongols.
● **During the reign of Iltutmish, the Mongol menace began to loom large over the Indian subcontinent** following Genghis Khan’s pursuit of Jalaluddin Mangbarni into the region. Iltutmish carefully navigated the situation by refusing asylum to Jalaluddin, thus avoiding immediate Mongol retaliation. However, **to** **strengthen his frontier and maintain strategic control, Iltutmish annexed Lahore and Multan—** **regions of great military importance**. These territories served as buffer zones against future Mongol advances and marked a deliberate attempt by the Sultan to consolidate power in northwestern India. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● **Ghiyasuddin Balban** adopted a hardline policy against both internal rebellion and external threats. One of his key administrative measures was to strengthen the defence of the Sultanate’s northwestern frontier **against recurring Mongol raids**. To this end, **Balban repaired and fortified key military outposts** **including Bhatinda, Sunam and Samana,** all located in present-day Punjab. These towns were of strategic importance and their fortification acted as both a deterrent and a first line of defence against Mongol incursions from the northwest. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Alauddin Khilji faced repeated and large-scale Mongol invasions** during his reign. To counter these threats, he implemented a comprehensive military reform and strategic urban planning. **Among his most** **notable measures was the construction of Siri Fort in Delhi,** shortly after a Mongol invasion led by Taraghai. The fort was intended as a military cantonment and administrative stronghold and marked a critical turning point in Delhi’s urban and defensive landscape. The fortification of Siri symbolised Alauddin’s long-term defensive strategy and resilience in the face of continuous Mongol pressure. **So,** **statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which of the following were introduced during
the Delhi Sultanate in India?
I. 	 Usage of iron-stirrup in warfare
II. 	 Paper-making technology
III. 	Persian irrigation wheel
IV. 	 Lime mortar for construction
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Delhi Sultanate (1206-1526 CE) ushered in significant technological advancements through Indo-Islamic synthesis. Various innovations were introduced during this period; Key innovations included the widespread adoption of the **true arch and dome** with strong **lime mortar**, revolutionizing architecture. **Improved Persian wheels (Rahat)** with geared mechanisms enhanced irrigation. **Paper-making technology** was systematically introduced and popularized, boosting administration and scholarship. In textiles, the **spinning wheel (charkha)** and **carder's bow** became common, increasing efficiency. Though rudimentary, knowledge of **gunpowder** also entered India, paving the way for future military changes.
● **The introduction of iron stirrups in India** is attributed to the Delhi Sultans, particularly the **Turks** and other Muslim warlords who arrived during the medieval period. These groups, including Turks and Afghans, brought with them the knowledge and use of iron stirrups, along with wooden saddles, which significantly impacted cavalry warfare and military tactics. **So, point I is correct.**
● The **paper** has a long history dating back to 3000 BC when Egyptians made writing material from beaten strips of marsh grass **“Cyperous Papyrus**.” The word “paper” derives from the Greek term for papyrus plants. The paper making process was further refined by T’sai Lun of China in 105 AD. The use of paper spread from China through the Islamic world and entered Europe in the early 12th century.
o The cotton textile and silk industry flourished in this period. Sericulture was introduced on a large scale which made India less dependent on other countries for the import of raw silk. **The paper** **industry had grown and there was an extensive use of paper** from the 14th and 15th centuries.
o Paper-making technology evolved by the Chinese and learnt by the Arabs was **introduced in India** **during the rule of the Delhi Sultans.** In India, the first paper industry was developed in Kashmir, **established by Sultan Zainul Abedin** (Shahi Khan) of Kashmir in 1417-67 AD. **So, point II is** **correct.**
● The **basic Persian Wheel (or Araghatta or Rahat) existed in India before the rule of the Delhi** **Sultanate.** However, during the Delhi Sultanate period, there was a significant improvement and **wider** **adoption of more efficient versions of the Persian Wheel**, particularly those incorporating geared mechanisms (like gear-driven Saqiya) that allowed for more continuous water lifting from deeper wells and sources. This technological enhancement greatly boosted irrigation capabilities. **So, point III is not** **correct.**
● The traditional basic units of construction in Ancient India consisted of clay, stones, wood and occasionally bricks. The simplest cementing material or mortar was plain earth mixed with water. **But** **lime mortar was brought by the immigrant Muslims during the Delhi Sultanate.** The basic ingredients in lime-mortar were lime (chuna) and surkhi (pounded bricks). The two major sources of lime were gypsum and gravel (kankar). **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Origin, Evolution, Geological Time Scale
questions.questionText:
With reference to the economy of the Delhi
Sultanate, consider the following statements:
I. 	 Sonargaon was a major Muslin trading centre
in Bengal.
II. 	 India became less dependent on imports of
raw silk due to the introduction of sericulture on a large scale.
III. 	Cambay in Gujarat was famous for textiles
and for gold and silver work.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The 13th and 14th centuries saw the rise and growth of several towns and cities in India. For instance, Lahore and Multan (modern Pakistan); Broach, Cambay and Anhilwara (western India); Laknauti, Gaur and Sonargaon (eastern India).
● **Sonargaon is one of the old capitals of the historic region of Bengal** and was an administrative center of eastern Bengal. It was also a river port. **Its hinterland was the center of the muslin trade in Bengal**, with a large population of weavers and artisans. According to ancient Greek and Roman accounts, an emporium was located in this hinterland, which archaeologists have identified with the Wari-Bateshwar ruins. The area was a base for the Vanga, Samatata, Sena, and Deva dynasties. **Sonargaon gained** **importance during the Delhi Sultanate**. It was the capital of the sultanate ruled by Fakhruddin Mubarak Shah and his son Ikhtiyaruddin Ghazi Shah. It hosted a royal court and mint of the Bengal Sultanate. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● **Silk** was discovered by Si lingchi, wife of China’s 3rd Emperor, Huangdi (Hoang-Ti), in 2640 B.C. While **sericulture (the practice of cultivating silkworms and extracting silk from their cocoons)** originated in China, **historical evidence suggests that sericulture and silk production existed in India much** **earlier than the 15th century**. It is believed that technology of sericulture reached India from China around 140 BCE through Tibet, initially flourishing in regions flanking the Ganga and Brahmaputra rivers. During the Delhi Sultanate, **sericulture was introduced on a large scale**, which made India **less** **dependent on other countries** for the import of raw silk. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Cambay in Gujarat was famous for textiles and for gold and silver work. India imported high grade textiles (satin, etc.), glassware, and horses from West Asia. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Prehistoric Period
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Inscription Known For
I. - Barah Copper Plate
Mentions of Mihir Bhoja’s military camp at Kannauj
II. - Deopara Insights on rule of Vijayasena
III. Vipparla One of the earliest known Telugu inscriptions
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Barrah Copper Plate inscription:** ○ **Mihir Bhoja** ascended to the throne in 836 AD and ruled for more than 46 years, becoming the most popular **Pratihara king.** He reorganized and consolidated the empire, ushering in an era of prosperity. ○ **Kannauj**, also known as **Mahodaya**, served as the capital of his empire, with the **Skandhavara** **military camp at Mahodaya** mentioned in the **Barrah Copper Plate inscription. So, pair I is** **correctly matched.**
● **Deopara Prashasti**: ○ **Deopara Prashasti** an important epigraphic source for the reconstruction of the history of ancient Bengal. ○ This inscription contains some eulogistic verses which throw important light on the history of **Sena** **dynasty**, especially Vijayasena. Famous Sanskrit poet of Sena period **Umapatidhara,** a minister of Laksmanasena, composed it. ○ The inscription refers to the reign of **king Vijayasena** and traces the genealogy of the Sena rulers of Bengal. ○ In verses 5-9, the Senas are described as **Brahmaksatriya** of external origin belonging to Karnata in South India. Vijayasena was portrayed as a **'great king' of ancient times** in verses 14-22. **So,** **pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Vipparla Inscription:** ○ The **Vipparla Inscription** is an important early record of the **Eastern Chalukyas**, specifically from the reign of **Jayasimha I**, who ruled in the 7th century CE. ○ This inscription is notable for being one of the earliest known **Telugu inscriptions,** marking a significant moment in the linguistic and cultural history of South India. **So, pair III is correctly** **matched.** ○ It reflects the gradual **shift from Sanskrit and Prakrit** to regional languages like Telugu in official and literary use. ○ Alongside the **Lakshmipuram inscription** of Mangi Yuvaraja, the Vipparla inscription showcases the early development of Telugu as a language of administration and record-keeping. **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● The **Ellora Record of Dantidurga**, the **Samangarh inscription**, and the **Navsari** **Grant** provide insights into the Rashtrakuta dynasty’s history.
● The **Gwalior Prashasti** and the **Barah copper plate** celebrate the achievements of Mihir Bhoja.
● The **Badal inscription** mentions the Pala ruler **Devapala’s victories** over various dynasties.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct
with regard to the social developments in the Delhi Sultanate period?
I. 	 The Hindus were considered zimmis for
which they were forced to pay a tax.
II. 	 The purdah system became widespread
among the upper class Indian women.
III. 	The Muslim society was divided into several
ethnic and racial groups.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● There was little change in the structure of the Hindu society during the Delhi Sultanate period. **The** **traditional caste system with the Brahmins on the upper strata of the society was prevalent**. The subservient position of women also continued and the practice of sati was widely prevalent.
● The **Hindus were considered** **zimmis** **or protected people for which they were forced to pay a tax** **called jiziya.** In the beginning jiziya was collected as part of land tax. Firoz Tughlaq separated it from the land revenue and collected jiziya as a separate tax. Sometimes Brahmins were exempted from paying jiziya. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The seclusion of women and the wearing of purdah became common among the upper class women. **The** **Arabs and Turks brought the purdah system into India and it became widespread among the Hindu** **women in the upper classes of north India**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● During the Sultanate period, the **Muslim society remained divided into several ethnic and racial** **groups**. The Turks, Iranians, Afghans and Indian Muslims developed exclusively and there were **no** **intermarriages between these groups**. Hindu converts from lower castes were also not given equal respect. The Muslim nobles occupied high offices and very rarely the Hindu nobles were given high positions in the government. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following best describes the
Itlaq system introduced by Feroz Shah Tughluq?

options.a: An assessment system for canal construction
options.b:
A revenue farming system where tax
collection rights were auctioned to the highest bidder
options.c:
A draft-based payment system for soldiers’
salary
options.d:
A postal system introduced to improve
communication across provinces

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In the Delhi Sultanate, efforts were made by each sultan to streamline the military administration. The Itlaq system by Feroz Shah Tughlaq was one among them. Feroz Shah Tughlaq gave up the practice of paying his royal soldiers in cash: instead, he gave them **a paper called** **itlaq** **- a sort of draft on whose** **strength they could claim their salary** from the Sultan's revenue officers of the khalisa ("Crown" or "reserve" land).
● In the Sultanate, the army consisted of troops maintained by nobles as well as the standing army of the Sultan. In the thirteenth century, the royal cavalry, in lieu of cash salary, was assigned the revenue of small villages in the vicinity of Delhi called "small iqta". Under **Iltutmish**, the **number of such cavalry was** **about three thousand**. **Balban** tried to **do away with these assignments** which led to much dissatisfaction. **Alauddin Khalji** was successful in doing so, and **he started paying his soldiers in cash**a trooper was paid 238 tanka while one who brought an additional horse used to get 78 tanka more. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Money (Evolution, Types, Digital Currency, Crypto, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Tax Levied On
I. - Kharaj Irrigation
II. - Zakat Property
III. - Kham Mines
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The different types of taxes charged and collected by the Delhi sultans can be broadly divided into two classes: religious taxes and secular taxes.
● **Kharaj was the most important tax**. The standard rate was one-tenth (1/10th) of the **agricultural** **produce**. Haqq-i-Sharb was an **irrigation tax** specifically introduced by Firoz Shah Tughlaq. It was levied at a rate of one-tenth (1/10th) of the produce on farmers who used water from the state-built irrigation canals for their cultivation. This tax was justified as the cost of providing the irrigation infrastructure. **So,** **pair I is not correctly matched**.
● **Zakat was tax on property realised from the Muslims.** It was a religious tax or **obligatory charity** **levied on wealthy Muslims.** The standard rate was typically 2.5% of their accumulated wealth and certain assets. The collected Zakat funds were meant for charitable purposes, helping the poor and needy within the Muslim community. Thus, Zakat was not a war booty. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Khams** or the **tax on mines**, treasure troves, etc. and the share in war booty. The tax on mines and treasure troves was 1/5th of the wealth secured. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Firoz Shah Tughlaq is known for abolishing many abwabs**, which were various illegal, oppressive, or miscellaneous cesses and taxes that had been arbitrarily levied by previous rulers. He is said to have abolished 23 such abwabs to relieve the tax burden on the populace like house tax, salt tax, etc., on the ground of their un-Islamic nature. **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Ziauddin Barani, a contemporary of Firoz
Shah Tughlaq, wrote Shahnama which outlines the political ideals and duties of a Muslim ruler.
II. 	 Abdul Malik Isami, a contemporary of
Ala-ud-Din Bahman Shah, wrote Futuhus-Salatin which is a poetic history of the Muslim conquest of India.
III. 	Ibn Battuta, a contemporary of Muhammad
bin Tughluq, wrote Rihla which outlines the socio-cultural developments in the Indian subcontinent.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ziauddin Barani:** He was also known as Barni and was a Muslim political thinker when **Muhammad** **Bin Tughlaq and Firoz Shah Tughlaq** ruled over the Delhi Sultanate. He is best known for his contribution to the historical literature and documenting events of the time. Barani’s **Fatawa-e Jahandari** presents a religious philosophy of history, influenced by Sufi mysticism, in which the events in the lives of great men are seen as **manifestations of divine providence**. **So, statement I is not correct.**
○ He believed that rulers like Balban and Firuz Shah Tughlaq, who upheld the duties of a good Islamic sultan, prospered, while those who strayed from religious precepts failed. His work explores the intersection of government and religion, offering both political theory and spiritual insight. ○ **Shahnama** is an epic of 50,000 couplets written by **Ferdowsi**. It poetically depicts Iran’s creation.
● **Abdul Malik Isami:**  He was a 14th-century Indian historian and court poet. He wrote in Persian language, under the patronage of **Ala-ud-Din Bahman Shah**, the founder of the Bahmani Sultanate. He is best known for **Futuh-us-Salatin (c. 1350),** a poetic history of the Muslim conquest of India.
○ Isami **dedicated** the work to **Ala al-Din Hasan Shah**, not Muhammad bin Tughlaq and he served under **Ala-ud-Din Bahman Shah**, not as a court poet to Muhammad bin Tughlaq. **So, statement** **II is correct.**
● **Ibn Battuta**: He was the greatest medieval Muslim traveler and author of **Rihla (Travels),** a celebrated account of his journeys totaling nearly 75,000 miles across the Muslim world and beyond, including China and Sumatra.
○ In India, Ibn Battuta was welcomed by **Muḥammad bin Tughluq**, who appointed him **grand** **qadi of Delhi**. Though well-received, he feared daily for his life under the Sultan’s unpredictable rule: **“an extraordinary mixture of generosity and cruelty.”** After falling into disgrace and narrowly escaping death, he regained favor and was sent as the Sultan’s envoy to China in 1342. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:** **Who were the other famous historians of medieval India?**
● **Abd al-Qadir Bada'uni**, was a 16th-century historian who wrote **"Muntakhab-ut Tawarikh"** **(Selections of Histories)** during the reign of **Mughal Emperor Akbar.**
○ His work provides a **critical perspective on Akbar's reign**, particularly his religious policies, and is considered a **valuable counterpoint** to the official court histories written by Abul Fazl.
● **Abul Fadl (Abul Fazl)** was a great historian of Muslim India. Also known as **Abul Fadl Allami**, some historians consider him the **doyen of Persian chroniclers.**
○ Trained in Arabic and Persian, Abul Fazl was regarded as an outstanding scholar, known especially for his **mastery of Arabic thought and philosophy**. His father, **Shaykh** **Mubarak**, a notable scholar himself, inspired him to pursue intellectual endeavors. ○ Under **Akbar’s patronage,** he composed many works, the most important being the **Akbarnamah** and **Ain-i-Akbari**. ○ The Akbarnamah is a **detailed account of Akbar’s life** and reign, taking seven years to complete. While the Akbarnamah provides a rich narrative of Akbar’s reign, the Ain-iAkbari offers a comprehensive account of Mughal administration.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Muhammad Tughlaq was the first Sultan of Delhi Sultanate who introduced token currency.
**Statement II:** There was a surplus of silver availability in India and throughout the world in the fourteenth century.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Muhammad Tughlaq** was the first Sultan of Delhi Sultanate who introduced token currency in 1330 AD. **So, statement I is correct.**
● He kept the value of token currency equal to silver tanka. The inscriptions on these token coins were in both Arabic and Persian languages. **There was a shortage of silver throughout the world in the** **fourteenth century.** Kublai Khan issued paper money in China. In the same manner, Muhammad bin Tughlaq issued **copper coins at par with the value of the silver tanka** coins. But he was not able to prevent forging the new coins. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● Muhammad Tughlaq **did not establish proper control over the issuance of currency**. The goldsmiths began to **forge** the token coins on a large scale. Soon the new coins were not accepted in the markets.
● Finally, Muhammad bin Tughlaq stopped the circulation of token currency and promised to **exchange** **silver coins for the copper coins**. Many people exchanged the new coins but the treasury became empty. According to Barani, the heap of copper coins remained lying on the roadside in Tughlaqabad. **So, statement – I is correct but Statement – II is incorrect.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:** **Who were the other rulers to undertake reforms of token currency?**
● Before Muhammad Tughlaq, **Kublai Khan** in China had successfully used token currency by introducing paper currency Chan (Chao) in 1260 AD. In 1294 AD, Mongol ruler **Gaikhat Khan (Gajan)** or Kaikat in Iran also used token currency
which was unsuccessful.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
City Founded by
I. - Jahanpanah Muhammad bin Tughlaq
II. - Hisar Ghiasuddin Tughlaq
III. - Agra Ibrahim Lodi
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Some of the Delhi Sultans took keen interest in building cities. Few of them are discussed below:**
● **Jahanpanah:** Jahanpanah—literally, **‘Refuge of the World**’—also called the fourth city of Delhi, was established in AD 1326 by the second Tughlaq sultan, **Muhammad bin Tughlaq.** Jahanpanah came to be established when Muhammad bin Tughlaq, the second Tughlaq sultan set out to establish a new city for himself in about **AD 1326. So, pair I is correctly matched.**
○ It is said that the sultan wished to unify the scattered urban settlements in the area, including the **old city of Lal Kot, the military cantonment of Siri, and the citadel of Tughlaqabad** into one city by enclosing them within a single walled area**.**
● **Hisar:** The city of Hisar was founded by a Muslim ruler, **Firoz Shah Tughlaq** in **1354 A.D**. ‘Hisar’ is an Arabic word which means ‘Fort’. The city, which we know today as ‘Hisar’, was originally called **‘Hisar** **Firoza (also Hisar-e-Firoza)** or in other words the **‘Fort of Firoz’**. But as the days rolled by, the very word ‘Firoza’ was dropped from its original name. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
○ It was in these lands that the **very first evidence of the presence of man** was discovered with the excavation of **Agroha, Banawali and Kunal**. All of these were the **pre-Harappan** settlements, bringing for us the very first images of pre-Historic times. The presence of the **pillar in Hisar fort** belonging to the time of Emperor Ashoka (234 A.D.) originally from Agroha, the discovery of coins of the Kushan Kings tells tales of ancient India.
● **Agra: Sikandar Lodhi,**  the ruler of the Delhi Sultanate, founded Agra in the year **1504**. After the Sultan’s death, the city passed on to his son, **Sultan Ibrahim Lodhi.** He ruled his Sultanate from Agra until he fell fighting to Mughal Badshah (emperor) Bābar in the **First Battle of Panipat** fought in 1526. **So, pair III** **is not correctly matched.**
○ The golden age of the city began with the Mughals. It was known then as **Akbarabad** and remained the capital of the Mughal Empire under the **Badshahs (emperors) Akbar, Jahangir** **and Shah Jahan.** ○ Akbar made it the eponymous seat of one of his original twelve subahs (imperial top-level provinces), bordering (Old) Delhi, Awadh (Oudh), Allahabad, Malwa and Ajmer subahs. **Shah** **Jahan** later shifted his capital to **Shahjahanabad** in 1649. **So, only one of the pairs given above is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:** **The story of other major cities of India:**
● **Tughlaqabad:** Tughlaqabad is considered to be the **third extant city of Delhi**, after **Lal Kot (built** **in mid-eleventh century by the Tomars) and Siri (built by the second ruler of the Khalji** **Dynasty, Alauddin Khalji).**
○ **Ghiasuddin Tughlaq**, during his short reign (AD 1320–24), built Tughlaqabad, the second fortified city, spanning across nearly 6.5 km, over a period of only four years.
● **Delhi:** Delhi, the capital of India, has a strong historical background. It was ruled by some of the most powerful emperors in Indian history. The history of the city is as old as the epic Mahabharata. The town was known as **Indraprastha,** where Pandavas used to live.
○ In due course eight more cities came alive adjacent to Indraprastha: Lal Kot, Siri, **Dinpanah**, Quila Rai Pithora, Ferozabad, Jahanpanah, Tughlakabad and Shahjahanabad. ○ Delhi has been a witness to the political turmoil for over five centuries. It was ruled by the Mughals in succession to Khiljis and Tughlaqs. ○ In 1192 the legions of the Afghan warrior **Muhammad of Ghori** captured the Rajput town, and the **Delhi Sultanate** was established (1206). The **invasion of Delhi by Timur in 1398** put an end to the Sultanate; the Lodis, last of the Delhi sultans, gave way to Babur, who, after the Battle of Panipat in 1526, founded the Mughal Empire. ○ The early Mughal emperors favoured Agra as their capital, and Delhi became their permanent seat only after **Shah Jahan built (1638) the walls of Old Delhi**.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Rang Mahal in the Bagh caves contains
murals depicting Buddhist Jataka tales.
II. 	 Upar Kot in Junagadh is a Buddhist vihara.
III. 	The Udayagiri-Khandagiri caves are known
for the sculpture of Narasimha (half-lion,
halfman).
How many of the statements given above are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Bagh Cave Paintings** Representing an **extension to the** **Ajanta school,** Bagh caves in Madhya Pradesh with their exquisite work rank quite close to the actual Ajanta caves in terms of their design, execution and decoration. The main difference is that the figures are more **tightly modeled, have stronger outlines,** **and are more earthly and human.**
○ **Cave No. 4**, known as **Rang Mahal**, has beautiful murals on the walls depicting **Buddhist Jataka** **tales**, just like those in Ajanta. **So, statement I is correct.** ○ Although scanty and decayed now, these paintings depict religious themes in the light of contemporary **lifestyle of people**, thus are **more secular** in nature.
● **Junagadh Caves:** These are **Buddhist caves** located in Junagadh district of **Gujarat**. In place of caves, three different sites can be found: Khapra Kodiya, Baba Pyare and Uparkot.
○ A unique feature of the Junagadh caves is the presence of **a 30-50 ft high citadel** known as **“Upar** **Kot”** in front of the prayer hall. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Udayagiri caves:** Udayagiri caves **(not Udayagiri-Khandagiri Caves in Odisha)** is located in **Vidisha,** **Madhya Pradesh**. Created in the early 5th century AD under the patronage of **Chandragupta II,** it is famous for having numerous sculptures on the hill walls.
○ The sculpture of **Varaha or Boar incarnation of Vishnu** is notable. The caves have one of the earliest Hindu sculptures.
○ It also has caves dedicated to Shiva, **Narasimha (half-lion, halfman), Narayana (resting** **Vishnu) and Skanda**. **So, statement III is not correct.** **So, only one of the above statements is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:** **What are the Udayagiri-Khandagiri Caves in Odisha known for?**
● Built somewhere around the 2nd century BC by **King Kharavela of the Meghavahana** **dynasty,** widely regarded as the most powerful king in the annals of history of Odisha, these ancient rock cut caves were first discovered by a young British Officer Andrew Sterling in the 19th century AD.
● The caves built on the **Kumari mountain range** were built for the **Jain monks** and offered them a place to stay and meditate. Out of the one hundred and seventeen caves that were built originally only thirty-three survive till this day. Eighteen caves are located in the Udaygiri hill and fifteen in the Khandagiri hill.
● The caves served as **residences for the Jain monks** and were equipped with a **water source** in the form of a small canal which passes through every cave, an ingenious communication system through holes, a place to light the lamps and the tilted flooring which served as a head rest. Many of the caves are double storeyed and it is believed that the upper chambers of the caves were used for deep meditation.

questions.questionText:
The terms ‘Rekha-prasad’, ‘Phamsana’ and
‘Valabhi’ are related to:

options.a:
Types of arches used in Buddhist cave
architecture
options.c: Decorative motifs used in Indo-Islamic domes

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Nagara-style temple architecture** is common in northern Indian temples, defined by the Garbhagriha, Shikhara (Curvilinear Tower), and Mandapa.
● In North India, temples are built generally on a **stone platform** with steps leading up to it. The plan of a Nagara-style temple is **four-sided**.
● While the Nagara earliest temples had just one tower or shikhara, later temples had several. ○ There are many **subdivisions of Nagara temples** depending on the shape of the shikhara i.e., **Rekha-Prasad or Latina, Phamsana and Valabhi.**
**Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** Other features of the Nagara-style temple architecture:
● **Garbhagriha:** The garbhagriha is always located directly under the tallest tower.
● **Jagati:** A Nagara temple generally rests on a high platform known as jagati over a small platform called pitha.
● **Adhisthana:** Rising above this is a smaller platform, which forms the base for the construction of the superstructure of the temple.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
School of Temple Building
Notable feature
I. - Vijayanagara Mythical creature ‘Yali’
II. - Hoysala Stellate plan
III. - Nayaka Prakarms
IV. - Nagara High boundary walls
V. - Dravida Vimana
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Vijayanagara School:** ○ The rulers of the Vijayanagara Empire (1335-1565 AD) were great patrons of art and architecture with capital at Hampi (Karnataka). They combined the features of Chola, Hoysalas, Pandyas, Chalukyas architectural styles. ○ Under them, the architectural style began getting influenced by the IndoIslamic style of Bijapur,
which in turn was reflected in the temples built during this period. ○ The features of the temples were: ■ The walls of the temples were **highly decorated** with carvings and geometrical patterns. ■ **Gopurams,** which were previously present on the front side, were now built on **all the** **sides.** ■ Monolithic rock pillars. ■ Generally, temple pillars had a **mythical creature Yali** engraved in them. **So, pair I is** **correctly matched.** ■ The enclosing walls were larger. ■ More than one mandapas were built in each temple. The central mandap came to be known as **kalyana mandapa** (dedicated to divine marriage). ■ The concept of **secular buildings** inside the temple premises was also introduced during this period. ■ The temple complex was enclosed by **boundaries.** ■ **Examples:** Vittalaswami Temple Complex, Lotus Mahal, Virupaksha Temple and Raghunatha Temple at Hampi, etc. The rock-cut idol of Narasimha on Shesha (snake) at Hampi is a marvel in itself.
● **Hoysala School:** ○ In the region of **Karnataka (near Mysore),** the temples built under the Hoysala rulers developed a distinct style of their own known as the Hoysala School of art. ○ It developed in the period from 1050-1300 AD with the prominent seats being **Belur, Halebidu** **and Sringeri.** ○ Some of the features of the architecture are: ■ **Multiple shrines** were built around a central pillared hall.
■ Unlike the crucified ground plan of the Panchayatan style, the shrines led out in the shape of an **intricately designed star**. This was known as the **Stellate plan. So, pair II is** **correctly matched.** ■ **Soft soapstone (Chorite schist)** was the main building material. ■ Massive emphasis was laid on the **decoration of the temple** through sculptures. Both the interior and exterior walls, even the jewelries worn by the deities were intricately carved. ■ All the chambers had **Shikharas** which were interconnected by an arrangement of horizontal lines and mouldings. This resolved the tower into an orderly succession of tires. ■ The temples were built on an upraised platform known as **Jagati,** which was about 1 metre high. The walls and stairs of the temple followed a **zigzag pattern**. ■ **Examples:** Hoysaleswara Temple at Halebidu, Chennakesava Temple at Belur, Chennakesava Temple at Somanathapura.
● **Nayaka School:** ○ The Nayaka School of architecture flourished under the Nayaka rulers in the period between 16th and 18th centuries AD. It was also known as **Madurai School.** ○ It was architecturally similar to the Dravidian style, but much larger in scope. It also had **Islamic** **influence**. ○ Some of the unique features are: ■ Presence of **Prakarms or huge corridors** in the portico, around the garbhagriha, along with roofed ambulatory passageways. **So, pair III is correctly matched.** ■ The **gopurams** built under the Nayaka rulers were some of the largest gopurams. The **Meenakshi temple in Madurai** has the tallest gopuram in the world. The art of gopuram reached its climax under Nayaka style. ■ The temple structure was filled with **intricate carvings**. ■ **Example:** Meenakshi Temple Madurai, etc.
● **Nagara School of Architecture:**  From 5th century AD onwards, a distinct style of temple architecture developed in the Northern part of India, known as the Nagara Style of Architecture. Even in the Nagara school, different sub-schools emerged in western, central and eastern parts of the country. Some of the features of Nagara style are:
○ The temples generally followed the **Panchayatan style of temple making**, which consisted of subsidiary shrines laid out in a crucified ground plan with respect to the principal shrine. ○ Presence of assembly halls or mandaps in front of the principal shrine. ○ Outside the garbhagriha, images of the river goddesses Ganga and Yamuna were placed.
● **Dravidian Style of Temple Architecture (Chola Architecture):** Under the patronage of the Chola rulers, hundreds of temples were built in South India. It was a continuation of the previous Pallava architecture, with some variations. This is what came to be known as the Dravidian style of temple architecture. The features of the Dravidian style or chola style are:
○ **Unlike the Nagara temples, the Dravidian temples were surrounded by high boundary walls.** **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.** ○ The front wall had a high entrance gateway known as gopuram. ○ The temple premise was laid out in panchayatan style with a principal temple and four subsidiary shrines.
○ Under Dravidian style, the spire is in the form of a stepped pyramid that rises up linearly rather than curved. It is known as **vimana**. **So, pair V is correctly matched.** **So, only four of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:** **Vesara School:**
● Also known as the **Karnataka School of architecture**, it was conceptualised under the later Chalukya rulers in the mid-7th century AD. It combined features of both the Nagara School and Dravidian school and resulted in a hybridised style.
● Some of its features are: o Emphasis on **vimana and mandapa.** o Open ambulatory passageway. o The pillars, doorways and the ceilings were decorated with **intricate** **carvings**. o Three prominent dynasties who made Vesara style temples were:
▪ **Chalukyas of Badami and Kalyani.** ▪ **Rashtrakutas** (750-983 AD). For example, Kailasha temple in Ellora, etc ▪ **Hoysala Dynasty** (1050-1300 AD). Temples at Halebidu, Belur, etc. o The influence of Nagara style is in the **curvilinear shikhara** and the square
base of Vesara temples. Influence of Dravidian style is seen in intricate carvings and sculptures, design of Vimana and step or terraced Shikara of Vesara temples. o **Examples:** Doddabasappa Temple at Dambal, Ladkhan Temple at Aihole, Temples at Badami etc.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the difference between Arcuate Style and Trabeate Style of Architecture:
I. 	 In Trabeate style, entrances are characterised
by the use of lintels, while in Arcuate style, they are characterised by the use of arches and domes.
II. 	 Unlike the arcuate style, the Trabeate style is
known for construction of Minars.
III. 	The top of temples in Trabeate style features
shikharas, whereas Arcuate style structures have domes.
How many of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Indo Islamic architecture** emerged during the medieval period, blending Indian and Islamic design elements. It introduced **the Arcuate style** with **arches and domes, minarets, and intricate geometric designs** like Arabesque and jaali work. It emphasized spaciousness, decorative calligraphy, and features like water pools and the **Charbagh garden layout**, blending artistry and symmetry.  **Trabeate** refers to an architectural form in which **horizontal beams (lintels) are supported by vertical pillars** to create openings.
● In Trabeate style, entrances are characterised by the use of **lintels**, while in Arcuate style, they are characterised by the use of **arches and domes.** **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Minars** were **absent** in the Trabeate style, but in Arcuate architecture, they were present on the four corners of mosques. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Stone** was the primary component used in Trabeate constructions, while **brick, lime plaster, and mortar** began to be used in Arcuate style buildings.
○ The top of temples in Trabeate style features shikharas, generally conical or curvilinear, whereas Arcuate style structures have domes, generally hemispherical, on top of mosques. **So, statement** **III is correct.**
**Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● **The Arcuate style** marked the introduction of arches and domes, replacing the **traditional** **Trabeate system** and giving architectural prominence to this curved structural form.
○ **Minars:** Islamic rulers introduced minarets surrounding mosques and mausoleums. ○ **Mortar Use:** Mortar became a primary cementing agent in Indo-Islamic constructions. ○ **Figurative Avoidance:** There was a prohibition of human and animal figures in architectural design. ○ **Spacious Design:** There was a shift from congested Hindu structures to more spacious and massive forms. ○ **Calligraphy Decoration:** Calligraphy was used for decoration instead of traditional sculptures.
● **Arabesque Techniques:** Geometrical vegetal patterns featuring continuous split stems were used for decoration.
○ **Geometric Principles:** There was extensive use of geometry for symmetry and decorative patterns. ○ **Jaali Work:** Intricate latticework signified the importance of light in Islamic architecture. ○ **Water Features:** Courtyard pools, fountains, and small drains were used for religious, cooling, and decorative purposes. ○ **Charbagh Style:** The Charbagh garden was introduced, dividing square gardens into four identical parts. ○ **Pietra-Dura Technique:** Decorative inlay using precious stones and gems was embedded into the walls for decoration. ○ **Foreshortening Technique**: A technique where inscriptions appear closer than they actually are was employed.

questions.questionText:
The term  ‘pietra-dura’ refers to which one of the
following?

options.b: A technique of inlaying semi-precious stones

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Pietra dura** (Italian: “hard stone”) is a **decorative art form involving the inlay of precisely cut and** **polished pieces of colored hard stones** to create intricate, illusionistic mosaic images. This technique flourished especially in Florence during the **late 16th and 17th centuries.**
● The term pietra dura refers specifically to stones with high hardness and durability, officially including those that fall between 6th and 10th degree on the Mohs scale (from feldspar to diamond). Common stones used in pietra dura include **quartzes,** **chalcedonies, agates, jaspers, granites, porphyries, and** **petrified woods.** These materials offer a wide range of natural colors, allowing for elaborate designs.
● **Lapis lazuli,** despite being slightly softer, was also commonly used, especially for its brilliant blue hue, but it does not technically fall under the pietra dura classification.
● Pietra dura works were primarily used to decorate tabletops and small wall panels, showcasing highly realistic pictures or decorative motifs. The process, known as **commesso mosaic work,** involved assembling stones into pictures much like a puzzle, resulting in durable and richly colored artworks. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
With reference to modern architectural styles in
India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Portuguese introduced the concepts of
Patio houses and the Baroque style.
II. 	 The Neo-Roman style featured a focus on
circular buildings.
III. 	The Indo-Gothic style extensively used the
curving arches.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Modern Architecture in India** evolved significantly with the arrival of European colonists, especially the Portuguese, French, and British, each introducing their own architectural styles.
● The **Portuguese** brought the **Iberian style**, initially building trading posts and warehouses, later transforming them into **fortified coastal towns**.
○ They introduced the concepts of **Patio houses** and the **Baroque style**, characterized by elaborate, dramatic designs with contrasting colours. **So, statement I is correct.** ○ Notable examples include Sé Cathedral (Goa), Basilica of Bom Jesus (Goa, Baroque style, World Heritage Site), Castella de Aguada (Mumbai), St. Paul’s Church (Diu, Baroque style), Diu Fort, and Church of St. Anne (Goa).
● The **Neo-Roman style** (post-1911) influenced construction in New Delhi, led by Edwin Lutyens and Herbert Baker, with features like anonymous designs, **circular buildings,** upturned domes, and a mix of oriental and western motifs. Iconic structures include the Supreme Court and Rashtrapati Bhavan. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● The **British** introduced the **Gothic style** which merged with Indian elements, resulting in the **Indo-Gothic** **(Victorian) style**. Key features were large, elaborate structures, thinner walls, **pointed arches (unlike** **Indo-Islamic curving arches),** large windows, cruciform church plans, and advanced British engineering using steel, iron, and poured concrete. **So, statement III is not correct.**
○ Famous examples include the Victoria Memorial (Kolkata) and Gateway of India (Mumbai). **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**What were the other developments in  Modern Architecture in India?**
● **Laurie Baker**, known as the “**Architect of the Poor**” and the “**Gandhi of Indian** **Architecture**,” revolutionized mass housing in Kerala.
○ He emphasized **environment-friendly construction**, the use of **local materials**, **filler slab construction** to reduce steel and cement usage, and focused on **ventilation and thermal comfort**.
● The **French** influenced **urban city planning** in India, evident in towns like **Puducherry** and **Chandernagore** (now Chandannagar, West Bengal) using **Cartesian grid plans** and scientific architectural designs.
○ The French introduced **anonymous architecture**, emphasizing simple facades without ornamentation, as seen in modern buildings. ○ Other French-influenced towns include **Mahe** (Kerala), **Karaikal** (Tamil Nadu), and **Yanam** (Andhra Pradesh). Notable examples are the **Church of Sacred** **Heart of Jesus** (Puducherry) and **Sacred Heart Church** (Chandannagar).

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the ancient Indian painting traditions:
I. 	 Lepya Chitra involves pasting colors on
fabric to depict myths and legends.
II. 	 Lekhya Chitra is typically made using dust or
powdered pigments.
III. 	Pata Chitra is a form of cloth-based scroll
painting.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Lepya Chitra** refers to a form of ancient Indian painting where myths and legends were depicted on textiles. This technique is frequently mentioned in **Brahmanical and Buddhist literature**, highlighting its cultural significance in early Indian art traditions.  Unlike Lekhya Chitra (line drawings or sketches), **Lepya Chitra specifically involved application or pasting of colors** to create visual stories on cloth or fabric. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **Dhuli Chitra** is an art made using dust, while the **Pata Chitra** is a painting on cloth or scrolls. **So,** **statement II is not correct and statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
With reference to Mughal painters, consider the
following statements:
I. 	 Abdus Samad and Mir Sayyid Ali introduced
Persian elements into Mughal painting.
II. 	 Bihzad illustrated the Mughal family tree
during Humayun’s reign.
III. 	Daswanth, Basawan and Kesu were prominent painters in Akbar’s court.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Mughal miniature paintings** are known for their **brilliant colours**, **precise line drawing**, and **diverse themes** (except for religious subjects).
● **Babur** had little time for painting but is believed to have patronised the Persian artist **Bihzad,** who made some illustrations of the Mughal family tree. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Humayun** was a significant patron and, during his exile in Persia, brought back artists **Abdus Samad** and **Mir Sayyid Ali**. These artists introduced Persian styles to India and produced illustrated albums. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● **Akbar** established a formal studio called **Tasvir Khana** where artists were salaried and encouraged to develop their own styles.
○ He saw painting as both study and entertainment, rewarding lifelike portraits. Indian artists from previous regimes were incorporated, giving rise to a distinct Mughal style with **3D figures**, continued **foreshortening**, and increased use of **calligraphy**. ○ Art became more focused on **courtly scenes** rather than popular themes. Notable artists included **Daswanth**, **Basawan**, and **Kesu**. **So, statement III is correct.** ○ Key illustrated works during his reign were **Tutinama, Hamzanama, Anvar-i-Suhaili, and** **Gulistan of Sadi**. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the developments in paintings during the Mughal
period:
I. 	 Paintings under Jahangir focused on
naturalistic depictions of animals and plants.
II. 	 Shah Jahan’s period witnessed the use of
gold, silver and brighter colours in paintings.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Under Jahangir**, Mughal painting reached its **zenith**. Jahangir was a **naturalist** who preferred paintings of **flora and fauna** such as birds, animals, trees, and flowers.
○ He shifted focus from illustrated manuscripts to **albums** and emphasized naturalism in **portrait** **painting**. **So, statement I is correct.** ○ A distinctive feature of this period was the use of decorated margins that were sometimes as elaborate as the paintings themselves. ○ Jahangir was regarded as a good artist and maintained his own private workshop, though no works directly by him survive.
○ His atelier was known for producing miniature paintings, particularly naturalistic studies of the zebra, turkey, and cock.
● **Under Shah Jahan**, the character of Mughal painting changed significantly. Moving away from the naturalistic style favored by his predecessors, Shah Jahan preferred more **artificial elements** and a sense of **stillness** in paintings, influenced by **European art**.
○ He discouraged the use of **charcoal** and encouraged the use of **pencil** for drawing and sketching. ○ Shah Jahan also promoted increased use of **gold and silver** in paintings and preferred **brighter** **colour palettes**. **So, statement II is correct.** ○ The Mughal atelier expanded during his reign, but the style and techniques saw notable changes, moving towards more decorative and less lifelike representations. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
School of Painting Associated Painting
I. - Kishangarh Bani Thani
II. - Mewar Ragamala
II. - Bundi Durgacharitra
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The history of paintings can be known from primitive rock paintings of Bhimbetaka, Mirzapur and Panchmarhi. They were followed by the painted pottery of the Indus Valley Civilisation, but the real beginning of the art of painting began from the Gupta Age. The earliest examples of miniature painting in India exist in the form of illustrations to the religious texts on Buddhism executed under the Palas of eastern India and the Jain texts executed in western India during the 11th-12th centuries A.D. Over the period various regional schools of paintings emerged in India with their distinct elements.
● **The painting of Bani Thani** is believed to have been created under the patronage of Maharaja Savant Singh (1699-1764) the seventh ruler of **Kishangarh** who ruled the princely state of Kishangarh from 1748 to 1757. The subject of the painting is thought to be **“Vishnupriya”**, a singer and poetess who was employed by Maharaja Savant Singh’s stepmother i.e Bankavatji as a singer in the palace. **The name** **"Bani Thani" translates** to **"adorned lady"** or "well-dressed woman" reflecting her elegant portrayal. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● The emergence of the **Mewar School** is widely associated with an early dated set of Ragamala paintings painted at Chawand in 1605 by an artist named Nisardin.  **Prominent examples of Mewar schools’** **paintings are: the Ragamala (1628)**, Rasikapriya, Bhagvata Purana (1648) and the Yuddha Kanda of Ramayana (1652). **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● The **Durgacharitra** is an important illustrated text focusing on the goddess Durga. The Durgacharitra series is strongly **associated with the Mewar School of painting,** particularly **commissioned under** **Maharana Jagat Singh I in the mid-17th century.**
o The **Bundi style of painting is very close to the Mewar style,** but the former excels the latter in quality. Painting in Bundi started as early as circa 1625 A.D. A painting showing **Bhairavi Ragini** **is one of the earliest examples of Bundi painting.** Some examples are an illustrated manuscript of the Bhagawata Purana and a series of the Rasikapriya. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Folk Music State
I. - Baul West Bengal
II. - Alha Uttarakhand
III. - Maand Himachal Pradesh
IV. - Hereileu Nagaland
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Indian folk music is a vibrant and incredibly diverse tapestry, reflecting the country's vast cultural, linguistic, and regional variety. Unlike classical music, it generally doesn't adhere to strict grammatical rules (like ragas and talas in their classical forms), but instead arises from the daily lives, rituals, and collective experiences of communities. It has historically been passed down orally from generation to generation, leading to regional variations and spontaneous improvisations. It's the music of the "common people" – farmers, laborers, artisans, and tribes.
● **Baul music, originating from West Bengal,** is known for its mystical and philosophical lyrics that blend elements of Hindu Bhakti traditions (especially Vaishnavism) and Sufi mysticism. **So, pair I is correctly** **matched.**
● **Alha music form is from Madhya Pradesh** and is a heroic ballad song with intricate words. It is **usually** **sung in different languages like Braj, Awadhi and Bhojpuri.** This form is also related to the epic Mahabharata as they try to glorify the heroes who are seen as the reincarnations of the Pandavas. **So, pair** **II is not correctly matched.**
● **Maand** folk music is from **Rajasthan**. It is said to have developed in the royal courts and hence is also recognised in the classical circles. The songs are usually about the bards singing the glory of the Rajput rulers. **It is considered close to Thumri or Ghazal.** **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
● **Hereileu** is sung by the people of Naga tribe in **Nagaland** to narrate their achievements, bravery, and experiences from past battles. **So, pair IV is correctly matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Sl. No** **Folk Music** **State/ Union Territory**
I. - Wanawan Jammu and Kashmir
II. - Panihari Rajasthan
III. - Lavani Maharashtra
IV. - Powada Maharashtra
V. - Khongjom Parba Manipur
VI. - Mando Goa

questions.questionText:
Consider the following festivals:
I. 	 Jawara
II. 	 Jhumair
III. 	Kaikottikali
IV. 	 Baisakhi
How many of the above are mainly celebrated for harvest?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Indian folk dances are performed on many different occasions such as rites of passage, changes of season and religious and cultural festivals.
● **Jawara** is the **harvest dance popular in the Bundelkhand region of Madhya Pradesh.** The dance,
which includes balancing a basket full of jawar on the head, is accompanied by heavy instrumental music. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Jhumair** is a **popular harvest dance,** performed by the tribal people of **Jharkhand** and Odisha. It has two variations – Janani Jhumair, performed by women and Mardana Jhumair, performed by men. It is a major attraction at many fairs and festivals. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Kaikottikali** is a popular temple dance of **Kerala**. It is performed by both men and women at the time of Onam to celebrate the rich harvest. Airukali and Tattamakali are similar forms of this dance. **So, point III** **is correct.**
● **Vaisakhi/Baisakhi** is a religious festival celebrated on 13 or 14 April every year. This festival is the celebration of sikh new year and birthday of Khalsa Panth. **This is the spring harvest festival** for the Sikhs. Gurudwaras are decorated and hold kirtans. Sikhs take a bath in the sacred river, visit temples, meet friends and party over festive foods. **So, point IV is correct.** **So, all four of the above festivals are mainly celebrated for harvest.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Sl. No** **Folk Dance** **State**
I. - Kalbelia Rajasthan
II. - Singhi Chham Sikkim
III. - Alkap Jharkhand
IV. - Paika Odisha
V. - Bihu Assam
